segments.s164-g9: خداوند here functions as a title 'the sovereign/lord (of ...)' and governs the following list of heavenly bodies. The sequence 'بهرام و کیوان و ماه' is a straightforward coordination (A و B و C) naming astral figures; word order is noun (head) then coordinated objects in apposition.
segments.s164-g10: The second خداوند repeats the title, now governing an abstract series 'نیک و بد و فر و جاه' (good and bad and farr and rank). The repetition is emphatic: 'the sovereign of X, the sovereign of Y...' The coordinated nouns are simple nominative/absolute nouns listed by و.
segments.s164-g11: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command (of...)'. اویست is a condensed/poetic form combining او + است (literally 'he/it is his' or 'it is by him'); here it links the prepositional phrase to the subject 'گردان سپهر' ('the revolving sky/sphere'), so the clause reads 'by his command is the revolving sphere.' گردان modifies سپهر ('the revolving sky').
segments.s164-g12: ازو is the old poetic form of 'از او' meaning 'from him' or 'by him'. بازگسترده is a participial/adjectival form 'spread/wide-open' describing what follows; هرجای functions adverbially 'everywhere' or 'in every place'; مهر here refers to the deity/realm 'Mehr' (i.e. the luminous/sacral power), so the phrase means 'from him the realm of Mehr is spread everywhere.' Word order places the agent/source (ازو) first for emphasis.
segments.s164-g13: ترا‌ای is a vocative plus dative 'to you, O ...' (ترا = 'to you' and ای is the vocative particle). جوان is the vocative noun 'young man' (addressed directly). تندرستی و بخت are two coordinated wishes/objects ('health and fortune') forming the things being wished for the addressee.
segments.s164-g14: همیشه 'always' sets the temporal scope. بماناد is an optative/subjunctive archaic form meaning 'may (it) remain' (wish/benediction). با + 'تاج و تخت' ('with crown and throne') states accompaniment/possession; تاج و تخت is a coordinated pair meaning rulership/high office. The whole is a formulaic blessing: 'may X remain always with crown and throne.'
segments.s164-g15: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بر دلت is a locative/target phrase 'upon your heart' (بر + دلت, with ت = 2nd-person suffix on دل). رای من is a genitive 'my counsel/opinion'; together the clause says 'if my counsel becomes dark on your heart.' تیره گشت is a simple past/resultative 'turned dark / became ominous.' Word order places the affected locus before the predicate (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s164-g16: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of'. خواب جوانی is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'the sleep (or slumber) of youth'. سرت is 'your head' (with 2nd-person suffix). خیره گشت is 'became amazed/stunned' (خیره = amazed/staring, گشت = became), so the clause reads 'from the sleep of youth, your head will become stunned.' The sequence places cause (ز خواب جوانی) before the result.
segments.s164-g1: هیونی (an object) + بیاراست (past, 3rd singular) yields a short clause meaning 'he adorned/outfitted the animal/beast.' The verb بیاراست is a classical perfective form of آراستن placed after its object. کاووس شاه that follows here does not belong grammatically to this small clause but introduces the next clause (the king who speaks/orders).
segments.s164-g2: بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb meaning 'he ordered/said.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), and بازگردد is the 3rd person singular present-subjunctive (classical use) expressing the commanded action 'return.' به راه is a directional prepositional phrase ('to the road') with به indicating motion/toward.
segments.s164-g3: نویسنده is the subject ('the scribe'); نامه را is the definite direct object marked by را. پیش here means 'in the presence of / before (them),' placed before the verb for emphasis, and خواند is the simple past 3rd singular 'read/recited.'
segments.s164-g4: به introduces the prepositional phrase expressing location/direction ('to/on'); کرسی زر is a noun + modifier ('a throne of gold' with classical noun-before-adj order); پیکرش ('his body') is the object with the possessive suffix, and برنشاند is the past verb 'placed/ seated' (here 'seat/ place [him]'). Word order places the prepositional phrase before the object and verb.
segments.s164-g5: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' used to introduce the action; نامه فرمود is a common classical phrasing where فرمود (past) indicates he issued/sent/caused the letter (here translated 'dispatched/sent a letter'). پر + noun means 'full (of)...' so پر خشم و جنگ describes the letter as 'full of anger and strife.'
segments.s164-g6: This is a verbless descriptive clause typical of Persian: 'زبان تیز' = 'a sharp tongue' (i.e. harsh/sarcastic speech) where زبان denotes 'speech/utterance' rather than the physical organ; و links the clauses; رخساره چون بادرنگ is a simile 'his countenance/complexion like bādarang (many‑colored),' with چون as 'like' and بادرنگ as the adjective 'multicolored.'
segments.s164-g7: نخست is the adverb 'first/at first.' آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'gave praise / praised' (آفرین here is a noun/phrase 'praise' used with کردن), and بر plus کردگار is the prepositional object 'upon the Creator'—a conventional formula meaning 'praised the Creator.' The simple past کرد is the auxiliary/carrying verb.
segments.s164-g8: خداوند here names the divine addressee/appositive 'the Lord/God.' آرامش و کارزار are two nouns in coordination describing attributes ('of repose/tranquility and of battle'), understood as genitive/appositional qualities of the Lord; there is no added verb in this noun phrase.
segments.s164-g49: سپه طوس (army of Tūs) is a noun‑noun construct (سپۀ طوس = 'Tūs's army'); رد را uses رد (radd, 'refusal') with the post‑position را marking it as the definite/direct object; نهادند is omitted here but the verb is the imperative pair of directives: ده (imperative 'give') followed by و بازگرد (imperative 'and return'). The order Verb Object is typical in Persian imperatives, and بازگرد is a second imperative ('go back').
segments.s164-g50: نه‌ای is an archaic 2nd‑person copular/negative formation here ('you are not'); مرد is the predicate noun ('a man' / 'a true/valiant man'); پرخاش روز نبرد is a genitive/attributive phrase: پرخاش ('assault/aggression') qualified by روز نبرد ('on the day of battle'). The whole clause means 'you are not a man who launches an assault on battle‑day.'
segments.s164-g51: تو is the 2nd‑person singular subject ('you'); با + خوبرویان is a prepositional phrase ('with the fair‑faced ones'); برآمیختی is past 2nd‑person singular of برآمیختن ('you mingled/consorted'), the prefix بر‑ is part of the verb and indicates the action 'to mix/associate.'
segments.s164-g52: به بزم اندر is a locative expression ('into/at the banquet') where به...اندر = 'into/within'; از رزم is an ablative ('from the battle'); بگریختی is past 2nd‑person singular of گریختن ('you fled'), so the clause reads 'you fled from the battle (having gone into the banquet).' The sequence contrasts location (banquet) with retreat from combat.
segments.s164-g53: نهادند is past plural of نهادن ('they placed/put'); بر + نامه‌بر is the prepositional phrase 'upon the messenger/envoy'; مهر شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's seal' (مهر here = 'seal' or official stamp). Word order is verb + prepositional phrase + possessed noun (the object of placement is given after بر).
segments.s164-g54: هیون 'beast/steed' is the subject of the following verbal sequence; پر here functions adjectivally/adverbially ('full/fully') and برآورد is a past form meaning 'caused to (move/advance)' or 'put into action' in this poetic context ('he set the beast full in motion'); و ببرید راه is coordinate: ببرید (past of بردن here 'he took/put to') + راه ('the road')—together 'and he took the road' (i.e., set off). The sequence describes mounting/speeding the mount and departing.
segments.s164-g55: چو is a conjunction 'when'; نامه 'the letter' is subject; به نزد is the locative/prepositional combination 'to the presence of' (here 'to/at'); سیاوش is the proper name object of that preposition; رسید is past tense 'arrived/reached.' The clause literally: 'When the letter reached Sīyāvaš.'
segments.s164-g56: بران is an adverbial/pronominal particle 'therein / upon that'; گونه گفتار 'a kind/manner of speech' is the noun phrase; ناخوب modifies it ('unpleasant/ill‑phrased'); دید is past tense 'he saw/found.' The clause means 'he saw that the wording/tone was unpleasant.' Note the compact poetic order (particle + noun + adjective + verb).
segments.s164-g193: 'اگر' introduces a conditional 'if'. The structure 'تیره‌تان شد دل' is literally 'your heart became dark' with 'تیره‌تان' (tireh + possessive -تان) modifying 'دل'. 'شد' is the past/become verb here meaning 'become' (resultative). 'از کار من' states the cause/ground ('because of / on account of my deed'), where 'از' functions as 'because of' in this idiom.
segments.s164-g194: 'بپیچید' is an optative/imperative-form verb with the b- prefix (here a command/request) meaning 'turn' (2nd person plural). 'سرتان' = 'your head(s)' with the plural possessor suffix -تان. 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from' or 'away from', and 'گفتار من' is 'my speech/words' — the whole phrase = 'turn your heads away from my words.'
segments.s164-g195: 'فرستاده' is a noun 'messenger'; 'خود' is emphatic 'own/oneself' (emphasising possession). 'باشم' is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative of 'بودن' used here to express 'may I be' / 'let me be' (or 'I will be'). The conjunction 'و' links the two predicates, and 'رهنمای' is a noun 'guide' that parallels 'فرستاده' (i.e. 'be messenger and (be) guide').
segments.s164-g196: 'بمانم' is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative of 'ماندن' ('may I remain' / 'I will remain'). 'برین' parses as 'بر' + demonstrative 'ین' = 'on that' (poetic/older form for 'on that'), so 'برین دشت' = 'on that plain'. 'پرده‌سرای' is a compound meaning literally 'curtain-house' → 'theatre/stage', used here to characterise the plain as 'stage-like'. The clause says the speaker will remain as guide upon that stage/plain.
segments.s164-g197: This is a temporal clause introduced by 'چو' = 'when'. 'سیاوش' is the subject, 'پاسخ' = 'answer', 'چنین' = 'thus/so', and 'داد' is the past tense 'gave'. The adverb 'باز' ('again/once more') follows and links to the next verbal result — a common poetic ordering where the adverb emphasizes recurrence.
segments.s164-g198: 'بپژمرد' is a past/result verb meaning 'withering/was made to wither' (classical formation with b-). 'جان' ('life/soul') is the patient/possessed noun, and 'دو گردن‌فراز' is a quantified noun phrase 'two proud-necked (men/ones)' — 'گردن‌فراز' = 'proud' or 'haughty'. Together: 'the life of the two proud ones withered.'
segments.s164-g199: 'ز' = 'from/because of' introduces the cause. 'بیم' = 'fear', 'جداییش' = 'separation of him/their separation' (جدایی + possessive suffix -ش), and 'گریان شدند' is a periphrastic past meaning 'they became weeping' → 'they wept'. The clause therefore means 'they wept out of fear of separation.'
segments.s164-g200: 'چو' = 'when'. 'بر آتش' = 'upon the fire', 'تیز' modifies the fire as 'keen/fierce', and 'بریان شدند' is the resultative past 'they were roasted/consumed' (بریان + شدند). The clause describes what happened to them when subjected to the fierce fire.
segments.s164-g65: همی before a past-tense verb gives a continuous or habitual sense (classical/poetic use), so همی گفت means ‘kept saying’ or simply ‘said’. The verb گفت appears before the subject (poetic inversion). صد modifies مرد (a numeral + noun phrase), and ترک و سوار is a coordinated noun phrase ‘Turks and horsemen’ (both apposed to or specifying the صد مرد).
segments.s164-g66: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning ‘of / among / from’. خویشان شاهی is a genitival phrase: خویشان ‘relatives’ modified by شاهی ‘royal / of the king’. چنین نامدار is a predicate adjective phrase ‘thus renowned’ describing those men.
segments.s164-g67: همه repeats for emphasis and distributes over the following adjectives: ‘all (of them) well‑wishers and all (of them) innocent.’ Noun‑like adjectives (نیک‌خواه, بی‌گناه) describe their disposition; the coordination with و links the two qualities.
segments.s164-g68: اگرشان is اگر + شان (the conditional particle if with attached object pronoun ‘them’), so the clause is conditional. فرستم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive form meaning ‘I send / I should send’ used in the protasis after اگر. به نزدیک شاه ‘to/into the king’s presence’ is a prepositional phrase indicating destination.
segments.s164-g69: نپرسد and اندیشد are negative and affirmative verbs in a simple present/imperative style: نپرسد = ‘he does not ask’ (here negative 3rd person), نه = ‘nor’, اندیشد = ‘he considers/thinks’. از کارشان is a prepositional phrase ‘about/ concerning their affairs/deeds’ where از marks the object of thought or inquiry.
segments.s164-g70: همانگه ‘at that very moment’ sets the immediacy. کند is 3rd‑person singular ‘he does / he will do’. زنده here is adverbial/qualifying (‘alive’), and بر دارشان is a compound: بر + دارشان where دارشان (دار + -شان) denotes the gallows belonging to/for them — together ‘hang them alive upon the gallows’. Word order places the temporal adverb and verb before the object phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s164-g71: به نزدیک یزدان is an idiomatic phrase ‘in the presence of God / before God’, with به + نزدیک functioning as a locative. چه is an interrogative/exclamatory ‘what’ (here introducing the question). پوزش is the object ‘apology/excuse’, and برم is 1st‑person singular subjunctive/present meaning ‘I bring / I make’ (classical use after چه to ask what one can do or offer); it expresses the speaker’s inability to know what excuse to offer.
segments.s164-g72: بد آید is an impersonal or evaluative present meaning ‘bad will happen / it will be bad’. ز کار پدر gives the cause ‘because of my father’s deed’, where ز = ‘because of / from’. بر سرم is an idiom meaning ‘upon my head’ = ‘to me’ (i.e., the misfortune will fall upon me).
segments.s164-g145: قباد is the subject (proper name). آمد و رفت are two simple past verbs in sequence linked by و (and/then), giving a narrative 'came and went.' گیتی is the direct object placed before the verb (typical Persian flexible word order) and سپرد is the past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' with قباد as agent. The clause thus reports three successive past actions by Qobad.
segments.s164-g146: ورا is an archaic object/pronominal form 'him' (here referring back to Qobad). نیز هم is a double emphatic 'also/too.' رفته is a past participle/adjectival perfect 'gone/ departed.' باید is a modal 'must/it is necessary' and is followed by شمرد, an infinitival/presumptive verbal form meaning 'to count/reckon' — the construction 'باید + verb' expresses obligation or necessity, so the whole means 'he too must be counted as gone.'
segments.s164-g147: تو‌ای is the vocative 'O you.' نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed.' زنگه شاوران is a poetic epithet in apposition: زنگه (here used metaphorically as 'ornament/ring/peal') governs شاوران 'of the advisers' — a classical poetic compound giving an honorific image ('ornament of the advisers'). The phrase is vocative and descriptive.
segments.s164-g148: بیارای here functions as an imperative 'adorn/deck (up)' addressed to the addressee (2nd person). تن را is the definite direct object marked by را ('the body' / 'your body'), with را immediately after the noun. به introduces an instrumental/means phrase 'with/by,' and رنج گران is a noun + adjective 'rânj (suffering, toil) + garān (heavy/weighty)' meaning 'heavy / burdensome toil.' Word order (verb ... object ... به + instrument) is typical of elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s164-g149: برو is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'go.' تا here marks the goal/destination in the sense 'as far as / to' (classical use equivalent to English 'to'), followed by به + درگاه 'to the court/royal presence.' افرسیاب is the proper name (Afrasiab). So the clause directs someone to go to Afrasiab's court.
segments.s164-g150: درنگی مباش is a prohibition: درنگی is a noun/Verbal-noun 'hesitation/pause' or a verbal form functioning as object, and مباش is the prohibitive 'do not be' (do not). Together they mean 'do not hesitate/linger.' و is the conjunction 'and.' منه is an older imperative-like negative causative ('do not let / do not give') used with سر به خواب 'head to sleep' — an idiomatic phrase meaning 'let the head fall into sleep' (i.e., fall asleep). So the clause prohibits lingering and sleeping; منه is somewhat archaic and here functions like 'do not allow.'
segments.s164-g151: گروگان 'hostage' is the first member of a compound subject. و links it to این خواسته 'this request/demand.' هرچ هست ('whatever it is') is a parenthetical clause: هرچ = 'whatever/whichever' and هست is the copula 'is/exists.' Together they qualify 'this request' as indeterminate. The whole segment lists the items being discussed (hostage and whatever this demand may be).
segments.s164-g152: ز before a noun here functions as a poetic preposition 'by/with/from' indicating means or cause; it is repeated for parallelism (ز دینار و ز تاج و تخت). دینار = money/dinar; تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' stand together for royal power/sovereignty. نشست is the past of نشستن 'to sit,' used metaphorically here to mean 'were settled/placed/obtained' or 'came to rest' (i.e., the matter was arranged or bought by means of money and promises of crown/throne).
segments.s164-g121: دو گیتی (literally “two worlds”) is the grammatical subject. همی is the archaic/poetic progressive particle meaning “continually/indeed” and precedes the verbal complex. برد خواهد is an inverted poetic ordering of خواهد برد (will take/ carry off) where خواهد functions as the future auxiliary; ز من marks the source/agent “from me.” Overall: “the two worlds will take (away) from me.”
segments.s164-g122: بمانم is first-person singular present/jussive (optative) of ماندن meaning “may I remain / I should remain.” به کام دل is a prepositional phrase “to/for the satisfaction (کام) of the heart (دل).” اهرمن is the proper name Ahriman (the Devil) and serves as the possessor of the desire. The phrase expresses a wish: “may I remain to Ahriman’s wish.”
segments.s164-g123: نزادی is a past (negative) form of زادن/زادَن meaning “did not give birth (to).” مرا is the object clitic “me” (here split in the token list as م + را). کاشکی (= کاش + کی) is the optative/regret particle “if only / would that.” مادرم is the subject “my mother.” Word order is poetic/inverted: literally, “did not give birth me — would that my mother (had not).”
segments.s164-g124: وگر is the conditional particle “if / but if.” زاد here functions as noun “birth/origin” (literally “the birth of death”), مرگ is “death,” آمدی is a past-tense verb “came” (poetic form used here for ‘comes’/‘befalls’), and بر سرم is a locative phrase “upon my head” meaning ‘upon me.’ Together the segment means “or if the birth/coming of death comes upon me.”
segments.s164-g125: که is the subordinating/relative particle “that / for that,” introducing an explanatory clause. چندین بلاها = “many a / several calamities” (چندین modifies بلاها). بباید کشید is a verbal construction: بباید (poetic inversion of باید + ب-) is the necessity auxiliary “must,” and کشید is the verbal root کشیدن here meaning “to endure / to suffer” (poetic usage). So: “that many calamities must be suffered.”
segments.s164-g126: ز گیتی is the prepositional phrase “from the world” (ز = from; گیتی = world). همی again is the progressive/poetic particle “indeed/ever.” زهر is literal “poison” used metaphorically for bitter suffering, باید is the impersonal/modal “one must / it is necessary,” and چشید (from چشیدن) literally “to taste” is used idiomatically “to experience.” The clause: “from the world one must taste poison (i.e. experience bitterness).”
segments.s164-g127: بدین گونه is a fixed phrase “in this way / by this manner” (بدین = by/with this; گونه = manner). پیمان is the noun “covenant / pledge.” که introduces a relative clause “that,” من is the subject “I,” and کرده‌ام is the perfect “I have made.” Together: “the covenant which I have made in this manner.”
segments.s164-g128: به یزدان = “by God” (به as the preposition marking the object of an oath). و links items “and.” سوگندها are “oaths/vows” (plural), and خورده‌ام is perfect “I have taken/partaken (of)” used idiomatically “I have sworn (these) oaths.” The whole segment states that the speaker has sworn the covenant by God and by oaths.
segments.s164-g33: در + noun here forms a prepositional phrase 'in/within'. بی‌نیازی is a substantive meaning 'not needing / self-sufficiency' and takes the complement marked by به (here به شمشیر) — 'not needing the sword'. جوی is from جستن and functions here as a verbal/nominal idea 'seeking / search' so the whole phrase reads roughly 'in seeking freedom from the sword.'
segments.s164-g34: به کشور is a locative phrase 'in the country/realm'. بود is the past copula 'was'. شاه را آبروی is a classical inversion where را marks the possessor/topic (شاه را) and آبروی is the predicate 'honour/reputation' — literally 'the king had (the) honour' i.e. 'the king's reputation was (in the realm...).'
segments.s164-g35: چو is the conjunction 'when'. The clause follows classical flexible word order: subject (طوس سپهبد) + verb (رسد). رسد is third-person present (used here as simple present/future) 'arrives / comes'. پیش تو is a postposed prepositional phrase meaning 'before you / in your presence.'
segments.s164-g36: بسازد is third-person singular of ساختن 'to make/do/arrange' — here 'he will act / arrange (things)'. چو باید = 'as is necessary' (چو = 'as', باید = modal 'it is necessary / ought to'). کم و بیش is an idiomatic adverbial pair 'less and more / more or less' modifying how he will treat تو (you) — i.e. 'he will treat you as required, sometimes less, sometimes more.'
segments.s164-g37: گروگان is the head noun 'hostage'. که داری is a relative clause 'that you hold' (که introduces the clause, داری = you have/hold). به بند گران is a prepositional phrase 'in heavy/precious bonds' with گران qualifying بند (heavy/valuable fetters). Together: 'the hostage that you hold in heavy fetters.'
segments.s164-g38: هم = 'also'. اندر زمان = 'at the (appointed) time / in time' (اندر = in, زمان = time). بارکن here is an imperative 'load!' (بار کن → compounded as بارکن in poetry). بر خران = 'onto the donkeys' (بر = onto, خران = donkeys). So: 'also, at the right time, load (him/them) onto the donkeys.'
segments.s164-g39: پرستار is 'steward / keeper'. وز is an archaic/poetic item here (likely a contraction/variant of a preposition/connector) whose precise sense in the provided inventory does not match this usage, so its sense is uncertain. خواسته is a noun or past participle 'request / that has been asked for'. هرچ هست = 'whatever it is'. Together the phrase names 'the steward and whatever he has asked for / whatever the steward requests.'
segments.s164-g40: به زودی = 'soon / quickly' (adverb). مر is the enclitic/dative 'to me'. آن را = 'that' + object marker (آن = that, را marks the definite object). به درگه = 'to the court / to the royal presence' (destination). فرست is an imperative 'send!' (فرستادن). Word order places the indirect object (مر) before the direct object (آن را) and ends with the imperative verb.
segments.s164-g105: برفتیم is a 1st-person-plural past verb 'we went/left'. The phrase بر سان باد دمان is a simile: بر + سان + باد = 'upon/like the wind' and دمان (literally 'skirt/hem', here 'the skirts/edges of the land or hills') completes the image; word order is poetic, with the simile elements placed together after the verb.
segments.s164-g106: نجستیم here is used in the sense 'we did not find / we could not get' (from the root جست/جستن 'seek/find'), taking در جنگ ایشان زمان 'time for their battle' as its complement; در is the locative 'in/for', and زمان is 'time'. The clause therefore says they lacked time to fight them.
segments.s164-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کشور سراسر is a postpositive modifier ('the whole country' / 'the country in its entirety'), and بپرداختند is a 3rd-person-plural past verb; here it carries the sense of 'paid/rendered/subjected' in a political/tribute context (poetic uses allow 'پرداختند' = 'they made/subdued/paid').
segments.s164-g108: This is a simple coordination: گروگان 'hostages' and آن هدیه‌ها 'those/the gifts' are the objects of ساختند 'they made/prepared/arranged'. ساختند is the past plural of ساختن 'to make/prepare', so the clause lists what they provided after taking the country.
segments.s164-g109: همه موبدان is the subject 'all the priests (mōbeds)'. آن نمودند is 'they showed/pointed out that' (آن = 'that' or 'the way in question') and راه ('way/path') is the direct object; the verb precedes the noun here by poetic inversion but the grammatical relation is 'they showed the way'.
segments.s164-g110: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result 'so that / that'. ما بازگردیم uses the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive 'we return' (reported purpose: 'that we might return'). زین here is the archaic/compressed form corresponding to 'از + این' ('from this'), but the lemma list given only lists 'saddle/harness' — none of those fits the actual connective use, so its sense is not in the provided options. رزم‌گاه is 'battlefield'.
segments.s164-g111: پسندش نیامد is an idiom 'it did not please him/was not to his liking' (پسند + object suffix -ش, followed by نیامد 'did not come/was not acceptable'). همی is a small emphatic/poetic particle, and کار من 'my action/plan' names what was displeasing. Word order places the verbal phrase before the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s164-g112: بکوشد is a 3rd-person-singular present/subjunctive or modal-form of کوشیدن 'to strive/endeavor' (here 'would cause/require striving'). The prepositions به ... و به ... link two nouns in a parallel object phrase: رنج 'hardship/suffering' and آزار 'pain/torment', and من 'me' is the experiencer; syntactically the clause explains the reason the plan displeased him.
segments.s164-g73: ور is the conditional 'if' introducing the clause. ایدونک is a small archaic/poetic particle meaning 'in this way' or 'thus' (here modifying the whole clause). جنگ is the object 'war' placed before the verb (poetic word order). آورم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive ('I bring about / I should bring about'), used in this conditional sense to mean 'if I should cause/wage war'. بی‌گناه is a predicative adjective 'innocent', describing the speaker and attached after the verb phrase.
segments.s164-g74: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' modifying the manner. خیره here is an adjectival/adverbial use meaning roughly 'stunned/heedless' (poetic). با introduces the accompaniment 'with', followed by شاه توران as a noun phrase 'the king of Turan' (name + place), and سپاه 'army' at the end; the prepositional phrase 'با شاه توران' attaches to سپاه (poetic inversion: the object 'army' comes last).
segments.s164-g75: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' or 'world‑ruler'. نپسندد is third‑person present (used optatively/future‑oriented here) meaning 'will not approve' or 'will not accept'. این بد 'this ill/evil' is the direct object, and ز من ('from me' / 'on my part') marks source or cause—together: 'the sovereign will not approve this evil from me.'
segments.s164-g76: گشایند is third‑person plural 'they will open' (here idiomatically 'they will open their tongues' = 'they will speak'). بر marks the target 'against' (بر من = toward me/against me). زبان انجمن is a genitive construction 'the tongue/speech of the assembly' (زبان used metonymically for 'utterance' and انجمن 'the council/assembly'), so the whole phrase means 'the assembly will speak against me.'
segments.s164-g77: وگر is the conditional 'and if / but if'. بازگردم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive 'I return / I should return' used in the conditional clause. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the king's presence' or 'near the king' indicating destination; word order is typical poetic placement of the adverbial phrase after the verb.
segments.s164-g78: به introduces the indirect object 'to' (the person who will receive something). طوس here is the proper name Tūs (the hero/general), followed by the title سپهبد (spahbed) in apposition. سپارم is first‑person singular 'I entrust / I will entrust' and سپاه 'army' is the thing being entrusted; the verb appears before its object (poetic order).
segments.s164-g79: ازو is a source‑marking pronoun 'from him/it'. نیز هم are reinforcing adverbs 'also / too'. بر تنم is a locative phrase 'upon my body' (بر = 'on, upon'; تنم = 'my body'—here the provided lexical senses did not include the noun sense for تنم, see senses). بد رسد is an impersonal/third‑person construction 'evil will come' or 'misfortune will befall' where رسد (third‑person present) carries the meaning 'befall/occur'; together: 'harm will befall my body from him as well.'
segments.s164-g80: چپ و راست is a paired adverbial expression 'on the left and the right' (i.e., on both sides). بد is the noun 'misfortune/evil' seen/experienced. بینم is first‑person present 'I see' (here perception of trouble). و پیش بد appends 'and before me (is) evil'—پیش 'before/in front', with the verb and copula omitted for compact poetic phrasing.
segments.s164-g97: چنین رفت بر سر مرا روزگار is an inverted, poetic order. رفت is past tense (‘went’) used idiomatically as ‘befell’; روزگار (the grammatical subject) comes at the end. مرا is the object pronoun ‘me’ (here split in the data as م + را), with را the object/definite marker; بر سر = ‘upon the head of’ (i.e. ‘upon me’).
segments.s164-g98: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause (‘that/for’). با مهر او = ‘with his love’ (با + noun + possessive pronoun او). آتش آورد بار: آورد is past tense ‘brought/caused’ and بار is object ‘a burden/weight’ (here a figurative burning burden); word order places the verb before the object as is common in poetry.
segments.s164-g99: گزیدم is 1st-person singular past (‘I chose’). بدان here is the fused/poetic equivalent of به + آن (‘to/for that’); the data split shows ب + دان — دان as the demonstrative piece is irregular in the provided senses. شوربختیم is a noun with the first-person suffix (‘my wretchedness’ / ‘my ill luck’). جنگ is the object (‘battle/war’). The whole segment means ‘I chose that wretched battle’.
segments.s164-g100: مگر here introduces a wish/conditional sense (‘if only’/‘unless’). دور مانم is adjective + verb: دور ‘far’ + مانم, which is 1st-person present-subjunctive/jussive of ماندن (‘may I remain’ / ‘I might remain’), expressing desire. ز links to the following genitive phrase چنگ نهنگ (‘from the claw/grasp of the whale’).
segments.s164-g101: به بلخ اندرون بود چندان سپاه is a locative existential statement: به بلخ اندرون ‘in the interior of Balkh’, بود is past ‘there was’, and چندان سپاه ‘so many / such a host’ quantifies the army. Word order is poetic (locative phrase before the verb and quantified noun after).
segments.s164-g102: سپهبد is the title ‘spahbed’ (a high military commander). چو functions as ‘like / as’ introducing a comparison or appositive, followed by the proper name گرسیوز; کینه‌خواه is an epithet ‘vengeful / avenger’ modifying the name. The phrase names and characterizes the commander.
segments.s164-g103: نشسته is a participial/predicative form ‘seated’ or ‘sitting’. The locative phrase به سغد اندرون ‘in the interior of Sogd (Sogdiana)’ follows, and شهریار (‘the king’) appears at the end by poetic inversion — functionally the subject of نشسته بود (was seated).
segments.s164-g104: پر از کینه = ‘full of hatred’ (پر ‘full (of)’ + از + کینه). با تیغ‌زن صدهزار = ‘with a hundred thousand sword‑fighters’ where با indicates accompaniment/possession and صدهزار quantifies تیغ‌زن (‘swordsmen’). Together they describe the king’s state and forces.
segments.s164-g41: تو is the 2nd-person singular address (poetic 'thou' form). شوکین is an adjectival/predicative word meaning 'eager/fond' and here characterizes the addressee. آویختن is a verbal-noun (infinitive/gerund) 'clinging/attachment' and is marked by the object marker را; بساز is an imperative (2nd-sg) of ساختن 'make/arrange', so the phrase literally orders 'You — be eager and make/arrange attachment.'
segments.s164-g42: ازین is از + این ('from/of this'); در here is concrete 'door/gate' used idiomatically for 'this matter/point'. سخن‌ها is the plural 'words/speeches'. مگردان is a negative imperative form (م‑ + گردانیدن) meaning 'do not make/turn' and is governing the following adjective دراز 'long' — together 'do not make (the) words long'. Note the poetic/inverted order (object/topic before the verb).
segments.s164-g43: چو here is the temporal/subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic use). تو is the 2nd-person subject. ساز جنگ is a compound where ساز means 'device/means/implement' and جنگ 'of war' — 'the implements/arrangement of war'. شبیخون کنی uses the 2nd-sg present/subjunctive کنی (کردن) with شبیخون 'night-raid' — 'you make/launch a night attack'.
segments.s164-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. خاک سیه is 'black/dark earth' (an adjectival phrase). رود جیحون is the noun phrase 'the river Jihun' (note classical order 'rûd + name'). The final کنی repeats the 2nd-sg verbal form and here functions causatively: 'you will (thereby) make the Jihun run black with earth.' The clause is hortatory/conditional continuation of the previous 'چو ... کنی'.
segments.s164-g45: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). سراندر is an adverbial/locative compound (classical usage) meaning 'within / in person / among the chiefs' (here 'in his person/within'). نیارد is an irregular/archaic form in the line; the lexical senses provided in the worksheet do not match the verbal sense required here, so the reading functions as a negative/archaic verbal form meaning roughly 'will not be (found/let be)' — i.e. 'the spahbed will not be asleep.' به خواب is the locative 'into sleep' or 'asleep.'
segments.s164-g46: بیاید is an optative/indicative verbal form meaning 'will come' / 'may come'. به جنگ تو = 'to/for war against you' (به + جنگ with the addressee تو as the opponent). افراسیاب is the proper name placed at the end as the agent: 'Afrasiab will come to fight you.' The clause is a simple future/prophetic statement.
segments.s164-g47: وگر is the conditional 'and if'. مهر داری is a present construction 'you have love/affection' (مهر = love, داری = you have). بران is a pronominal/adverbial phrase 'for/on that / toward that' (i.e. 'toward him/that one'), and اهرمن is the proper name 'Ahriman' (the devil). The phrase = 'And if you have affection for that Ahriman...'.
segments.s164-g48: نخواهی is the negative of خواستن used here to express 'you will not/would not wish'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. خواندت is an archaic clitic/verb form (classical spelling of خواندَت / خوانَدْت) here best read as 'he will call you' (i.e. 'address you as'); پیمان‌شکن is the predicate noun 'oath‑breaker' (one who breaks covenants). Together: 'you would not wish that he call you an oath‑breaker.'
segments.s164-g81: نیاید is a classical negative 3rd-person verb meaning 'does not come' placed at the start of the clause (verb-first word order is common in poetry). ز + سودابه خود is a prepositional phrase 'from Sudabeh herself' where خود is emphatic/reflexive 'herself' (خویش split as خوی + ش in other lines). جز introduces the exception 'except' and بدی is the excluded thing 'evil/harm'; together the phrase reads literally 'does not come from Sudabeh herself except evil' = 'nothing but evil comes from Sudabeh herself.'
segments.s164-g82: ندانم is the first-person negative present 'I do not know.' چه is the interrogative 'what.' خواهد here is the future auxiliary (will) and رسید is the main verb 'arrive/come' in past form used with خواهد to form a future periphrasis ('will come/bring'). ایزدی is an adjective 'divine' used substantively ('a divine thing' or 'that which is divine') or attributively to mean 'what the divine will send.'
segments.s164-g83: دو تن را is a definite/direct-object or topical phrase 'two persons' marked with را (here presenting the two men). The following prepositional/genitive chain ز لشکر ز کندآوران locates them 'from the army of the کندآوران' (poetic repetition of ز links layers of specification). This is a classical, somewhat compressed genitive construction: 'two men from the army of the Kandāvarān.'
segments.s164-g84: چو ... و چون ... are comparative particles 'such as/like' introducing examples. بهرام is a proper name (Bahram) and شاوران is the plural noun 'counselors/advisers'; زنگه appears to be a proper name here (Zangah) but is not listed in the provided senses. The sequence therefore gives examples: 'like Bahram and like Zangah, the counselors.'
segments.s164-g85: بران is an adverbial/particle 'thereupon' or 'on that' linking to the previous action. رازشان (راز + -شان) is the object 'their (or his/their) secret' and خواند is the simple past 3rd-person verb 'he called/summoned/told' (in poetry خواند can mean 'summoned' or 'revealed'). نزدیک خویش is an adverbial phrase 'near himself' where خویش is split as خوی + ش (خوی = kinship/disposition root used here as 'own', ش = 'his'), so the clause means 'he thereupon called them and told his secret close to himself.'
segments.s164-g86: بپرداخت is a past-form verb here meaning 'he proceeded/turned/attended to' (an intransitive or inchoative reading 'he went/advanced to'). ایوان is the place 'the iwan/porch/hall.' و links the clauses, and بنشاند is an archaic past-causative 'he caused to sit / seated (them)' (بنشاند commonly appears in epic as 'he seated [them]'). پیش functions adverbially 'before/in the presence of (him).' The object 'them' is understood from earlier context.
segments.s164-g87: که introduces a clause 'that/for' explaining the reason. رازش is 'his secret' (راز + -ش). به هم is a fixed expression 'together / mutually' (here 'shared'), with به as the prepositional element and هم carrying the 'together' sense. بود is the past copula 'was.' با هر دو تن is the prepositional phrase 'with both persons' (با = 'with', هر دو = 'both', تن = 'person'). The whole clause: 'that his secret was together/held in common with both men.'
segments.s164-g88: ازان پس که is a temporal phrase 'from that time/after that when...' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then', که = 'when/that'). رستم شد از انجمن uses شد (past of شدن) in a narrative sense 'Rostam went/was/left' with از انجمن 'from the assembly' specifying departure; idiomatically 'when Rostam left the assembly.' The clause gives the temporal point: 'from that time when Rostam left the gathering.'
segments.s164-g25: ترا‌گر is the classical conditional 'ترا گر' = 'if you' (an inversion of گر after a personal pronoun). فریبد (فریفت/فریب) is the verb 'to deceive' used with the negative subordinating نباشد (a negative present/subjunctive/optative form of بودن here meaning 'are not / be not'), so the clause reads 'if you are not deceitful / if you do not deceive'. شگفت is an attributive/predicative adjective 'astonishing, strange' that frames what follows (i.e. 'my astonishment'); it functions as a comment on the following clause rather than as a separate main verb.
segments.s164-g26: مرا از خود اندازه باید گرفت is an impersonal/modal construction: باید + گرفت (literally 'must take') yields 'one must take/measure'. مرا (م + را) is the object 'me' (را is the definite/object marker attached to the pronoun), از + خود = 'from/of oneself' ('by my own self'), and اندازه here is the noun 'measure/measure/degree' used with گرفتن 'to take/measure'. Together: 'one must take the measure of me from myself' = 'judge my wonder by my own nature.'
segments.s164-g27: نرفت is the past 3rd-person verb 'did not go' used impersonally to mean 'there was not / did not occur'. ایچ (classical) = 'anything / at all' functioning as an emphatic negator here ('not at all'). با من سخن ز آشتی = 'a word with me about reconciliation' (با + me + سخن + ز + آشتی), so the whole segment means 'there was not at all any talk with me of reconciliation.'
segments.s164-g28: ز فرمان من = 'from my command / from my order' (ز = poetic 'from/of'). روی برگاشتی is a classical expression meaning 'you turned your face away' (روی = 'face'; برگاشتی here describes the action of turning away/disregarding). The verb برگاشتی is 2nd-person past (you ...), so the whole clause says 'you turned away from my command' (i.e. you disobeyed or ignored my order).
segments.s164-g29: همان رستم = 'that very Rostam' (همان as emphatic/demonstrative). از گنج آراسته is a prepositional participial phrase 'adorned with treasure' (از = 'with/of', گنج = 'treasure', آراسته = past participle 'adorned'), qualifying Rostam as richly equipped.
segments.s164-g30: نخواهد شدن is a modal/future-like periphrasis: نخواهد (will / will not) + شدن (become) yields 'will not become / will not be'. سیر is the noun 'satiety / being satisfied', and از خواسته = 'from/of what is desired' (خواسته = 'wish, desire'). Together the clause means 'he will not become sated from his desires' i.e. 'he will not be satisfied by what he wants.'
segments.s164-g31: ازان (from that) + مردری (مَردری = 'manliness, bravery') marks the source: 'from that bravery'. تاج شاهنشهی is a compound 'the crown of kingship/imperial crown' (تاج = 'crown', شاهنشهی = 'of kingship/imperial'), so this noun phrase means 'the imperial crown from that valor' (i.e. the crown came as a result of that valor).
segments.s164-g32: ترا شد is 'it became to you / it came to you' (ترا = dative 'to you', formed here as ت + را; شد = 'became'). سر از جنگ جستن تهی is a nominalized idiom: سر = 'inner mind/impulse', از جنگ جستن = 'the act of seeking battle' (جستن = verbal noun 'seeking'), and تهی = 'empty/devoid'. So the resulting clause means 'you became empty of the urge to seek war' (i.e. you ceased to desire seeking battle).
segments.s164-g57: فرستاده را: فرستاده is the definite object 'the messenger' and را is the object-marker attached after the noun. خواند is a past-tense verb 'he called/summoned' (classical use). و پرسید links a second past action 'and he asked'; پرسید introduces the reported question. چست is an archaic/question form (equivalent to modern چه است / چیست) asking 'what/who is (he)?' and functions as the content of پرسید.
segments.s164-g58: ازو 'from him' (prepositional phrase) supplies the source of what follows. کرد here is the past-tense of کردن used causatively/resultatively: 'he got/obtained/made (it so)'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/wholly' modifying سخنها 'the words/accounts'; درست is an adjective/adverb 'correct/accurate' describing those words. Word order in classical Persian places these modifiers after or around the verb more freely than in modern Persian.
segments.s164-g59: بگفت is a past-tense reporting verb 'he said'. آنک is a subordinating/relative particle 'that' (introducing the reported clause). با + پیلتن is the prepositional phrase 'with Piltan'; رفته بود is the pluperfect construction 'had gone' (رفته, past participle, + بود auxiliary) indicating an action completed before the time of reporting.
segments.s164-g60: ز طوس و ز کاووس are prepositional phrases 'concerning/from Tūs and from Kāvūs' (ز used in classical Persian where modern Persian would use از or درباره). کاشفته بود is a perfect/passive-like form: کاشفته (a past participle/derived form meaning 'revealed/uncovered') with the auxiliary بود yields 'had been revealed' or 'had been made known'. The clause thus states that matters about Tūs and Kāvūs had become known.
segments.s164-g61: سیاوش is the subject/name. چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is the past-tense verb 'he heard'. گفتار اوی is the object 'his speech/words' where اوی is the archaic possessive 'his' placed after the noun; literal order is Subject + temporal marker + verb + object.
segments.s164-g62: ز (here 'because of/about') introduces the cause/topic: ز رستم 'concerning Rostam'. غمی گشت is a causative/resultative expression: غم (grief) + گشت (became) = 'became sorrowful' or 'grief arose (in him)'. و connects the next action. برتافت روی is a verbal phrase: برتافت (classical compound meaning 'turned away/removed') and روی 'face' — together 'he turned his face away' (i.e., averted his gaze).
segments.s164-g63: ز indicates cause 'because of' followed by کار پدر 'the father's deed' (کار is head noun, پدر in genitive). دل 'heart' is the affected noun, and پراندیشه is a compound adjective 'full of thought/pensive; worried'. کرد (made/did) functions causatively/resultatively here: 'he made his heart pensive' → 'he became deeply worried'.
segments.s164-g64: ز ترکان و از روزگار are two parallel prepositional phrases giving additional causes/reasons: 'because of the Turks and because of the times/fate'. نبرد is a nominative noun 'battle/struggle' that here summarizes the concern (i.e., worries about fighting/war). The phrase as a whole lists the matters that filled him with anxiety.
segments.s164-g129: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). سر بگردانم is a first-person present/subjunctive-form verb phrase (سر 'head' + بگردانم 'I turn') meaning 'I turn away' — a poetic way to say 'I turn my attention/face away'. از راستی places the source/point of departure ('from truth/uprightness'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: conditional particle + object + verb + source.
segments.s164-g130: فراز آید is a poetic inversion where فراز ('up/above, forth') precedes the verb آید ('comes'); the logical subject (کاستی 'shortcoming, loss') follows at the end. از هر سوی marks origin 'from every side/direction'. Overall: 'a shortcoming will come forth from every direction.' آید is third-person singular present/predictive.
segments.s164-g131: پراگنده شد is a past tense predicate 'became widespread/spread'. در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world'. این سخن is the topicalized subject 'this saying' placed after the predicate for poetic emphasis (inversion common in poetry).
segments.s164-g132: که introduces the reported clause 'that …'. با here functions as an agent/instrument marker ('by') in a passive-like poetic construction. شاه ترکان = 'the king of the Turks' (شاه 'king' + ترکان 'Turks'). فگندیم is the past first-person-plural of فکندن ('to cast/throw'); in this line it carries a passive or resultative sense ('we were cast/thrown'). بن ('to the base/ground') is the endpoint/complement. The clause thus describes being cast down by the Turk king.
segments.s164-g133: زبان برگشایند is a verb phrase meaning 'they open their tongues / they speak out' — برگشایند is a third-person plural (optative/indicative) form used here to report others' actions. هر کس quantifies 'each person / whoever'. به بد is an adverbial phrase 'in an evil/ill way' (به + adjective = manner). The clause means 'everyone will speak ill (of me)'.
segments.s164-g134: به هرجای is an adverbial 'to/at every place' (i.e., wherever they go). بر من marks the target 'against me'. چنان چون سزد is a fixed expression: چنان 'so/such' + چون 'as' + سزد (third-person singular impersonal of سَزیدن) 'it befits / is fitting', together 'in whatever manner is fitting' or 'as each deems proper'. The clause: 'wherever, they will act/speak against me as they please/see fit.'
segments.s164-g135: This is a string of verbal nouns/infinitives describing wrongful actions. به کین ('in/with hatred') gives motive; بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return/revert' (here 'to revert to enmity/hatred'). بریدن is the infinitive 'to cut/sever'. ز (poetic form of از) + دین indicates the object 'from religion/faith' — 'to sever from the faith.' The construction lists sins or reproachable acts.
segments.s164-g136: کشیدن is the infinitive 'to pull, draw, or take'. سر here is 'head' and is used metaphorically/hyperbolically; from + آسمان و زمین ('from heaven and earth') gives the figurative scope/objects. Together the phrase is strongly idiomatic/poetic — literally 'to draw the head from heaven and earth' — i.e., an extreme, hyperbolic image of a disruptive or ruinous act. Word order is canonical for a list of infinitives in Persian poetry.
segments.s164-g17: شنیدی is the simple past second‑person singular 'did you hear'. که introduces a reported content clause ('that/what ...') whose internal word order is subject (دشمن) + indirect object/target (به ایران) + interrogative چه + verb کرد — together forming the indirect question 'what the enemy did to Iran'.
segments.s164-g18: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. پیروز شد is past tense 'became victorious' (پیروز + auxiliary شد). روزگار نبرد is a poetic noun phrase 'the fortunes/time of battle' (روزگار + noun modifier نبرد) and the subject affected by چُو is understood from the previous clause (i.e. the enemy); the clause means 'when the fortunes of war made him victorious'.
segments.s164-g19: کنون is the adverb 'now'. خیره آزرم is a vocative/nominal phrase (خیره 'staring/astonished' with آزرم as a proper name) used to address someone; word order is poetic. دشمن مجوی is a negative imperative: مجوی is the second‑person negative imperative 'do not seek/search for', with دشمن as its object and the subject 'you' omitted.
segments.s164-g20: برین is the preposition + demonstrative 'بر + این' meaning 'to/at this'; بارگه is 'court/palace'. بر مبر is a stacked preposition + negative imperative where مبر is 'do not take' and the preceding بر emphasizes direction ('to/onto'); آبروی is the object 'reputation/honor'. The phrase gives the command 'to this court do not take (away) your honor' (poetic word order).
segments.s164-g21: منه here is an archaic/poetic negative particle meaning 'do not' (imperative). با جوانی 'with/because of youth' marks the associate/condition, and سر اندر فریب is an idiomatic phrase 'head into deception' (سر 'head' used metonymically for oneself; اندر فریب 'into deception'). The clause is an imperative warning: 'do not put your head into deceit because of youth.'
segments.s164-g22: گر is the conditional 'if'. از introduces the agent/source 'from', and چرخ‌گردان is a poetic epithet for Fate/the revolving heavens. نخواهی is the second‑person present/future of 'to want' (here 'you would not wish' / 'you do not want') and نهیب is the noun 'a rebuke' (object). Together: 'if you do not wish a rebuke from the turning heavens...' (the clause is conditional and links back to the warning).
segments.s164-g23: که here acts as a causal 'for/because'. من is the subject 'I'. زان is the poetic 'از + آن' meaning 'from that / because of that' and links causally to what follows: فریبنده گفتار او is 'the deceiving speech of his' with فریبنده (adjective) modifying گفتار and او as the possessive 'his'.
segments.s164-g24: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/greatly' (intensifier) placed before the verb. بازگشتم is the first‑person past 'I returned/withdrew'. ز پیکار او is a prepositional phrase 'from his battle/fight' ('ز' = from, پیکار = battle, او = his). Altogether: 'I greatly withdrew from his fighting.'
segments.s164-g113: به marks the target/direction of the action ('against' or 'toward'); خیره is used as a noun/adjective meaning 'the stunned/astoned ones' and functions as the target of the action; همی is the epic/simple continuous/emphatic particle (found throughout Ferdowsi) placed before the verb to give ongoing or emphatic colouring; جنگ is the object 'war/battle'; فرمایدم is the first-person past (archaic/polite) form of فرماییدن here meaning 'I commanded/ordered'. The whole segment therefore literally reads 'I commanded war against the stunned.'
segments.s164-g114: بترسم is the first-person present (modal) of ترسیدن 'I fear'; که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); سوگند is the noun 'an oath, a vow'; بگزایدم is an archaic/poetic first‑person form (here functioning as a potential/subjunctive) meaning roughly 'I may be led to take/give' (i.e. 'I might be driven to swear'). Together the clause says 'for I fear that I may be forced to swear an oath.'
segments.s164-g115: وراگر is a compound/conjunctive particle meaning 'and if' / 'but if' that introduces a conditional; ز بهر is the prepositional phrase 'for the sake of' (ز here has a poetic 'from/because/of' sense combined with بهر 'for'); فزونیست is an archaic copula construction meaning 'is for increase/gain' (فزونی = increase, -ست = is); جنگ is the subject 'war'. Word order is typical older Persian: conditional particle + prepositional reason phrase + predicate + subject.
segments.s164-g116: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when' (or 'if'); گنج آمد and کشور آمد are two parallel nominal + verb clauses 'treasure came' and 'realm/country came' (آمد = 'came', used here in the sense 'fell into someone's hands' or 'was taken'); و links them; به چنگ ('into the claw/grasp') is a prepositional phrase indicating that they came 'into (one's) grasp' — چنگ here is metaphorical 'claw/hand/grasp'. The sequence is a typical Old Persian clause: temporal particle + noun + آمد + ... + به + noun (locative/figurative).
segments.s164-g117: چه here is a rhetorical interrogative 'what/why' (i.e. 'why should...'); باید is the modal 'must/ought' used rhetorically; همی is the habitual/epic particle adding ongoing/emphatic sense; خیره modifies the (implicit) agent as 'dumbstruck/staring'; خون ریختن is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase 'to shed/spill blood' (ریختن = 'to pour/spill', used causatively of bloodshed). The phrase asks rhetorically why one ought to be shedding blood while stunned.
segments.s164-g118: چنین is anadverb/pronominal 'thus/so' linking to the preceding action; دل is 'the heart' (the seat of feelings); به کین is a prepositional phrase 'to/for vengeance' (کین = vengeance/hatred); اندر is a locative 'in/within' used here with آویختن; آویختن is the infinitive 'to cling to / to attach oneself to' (here 'to hang the heart on/attach the heart to vengeance'). The whole expression describes fixing one's heart upon revenge.
segments.s164-g119: همی again is the continuous/emphatic particle; سر is 'head' used idiomatically for pride or defiance; ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from God' (here meaning 'against God' or 'in opposition to the divine'); نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'must not/one ought not to'; کشید is the infinitive/verb 'to draw/pull/raise' (کشیدن in older usage can mean 'to raise' or 'to draw out' — here 'to lift one's head (against)'). The construction is a prohibition: 'one must not raise one's head against God.'
segments.s164-g120: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'abundant/much' modifying the following noun; نکوهش is 'reproach/condemnation'; بباید is an impersonal modal 'one must/one will have to' (here implying consequence/expectation); شنید is the infinitive 'to hear' (used impersonally: 'one will hear much reproach'). The clause states the expected result of defiance: abundant blame will be heard.
segments.s164-g161: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn' and modifies the imperative برو ('go'). برو is an imperative/command 'go' (here directed at attendants). بر is the preposition 'to/toward' preceding the proper name شمر (Shamr). هرچ هست is a short relative clause 'whatever there is' (هرچ = هر چه, هست = 'is') that functions as the object/extent of what should be taken/given.
segments.s164-g162: ز repeated before each item is the poetic preposition 'of/from' marking the contents to be given or taken (here: possessions). گنج, تاج, تخت are a noun series 'treasure, crown, throne' listing valuables; و simply coordinates them. نشست here is a nominal/verbal kernel meaning roughly 'seat'/'sitting' (poetic use referring to the throne-seat or the throne's possession) that completes the list of what is to be handed over.
segments.s164-g163: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the subject (proper name). بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. گفتار اوی is a noun phrase 'his speech/words' (گفتار = 'speech', اوی = archaic possessive 'his'), the object of بشنید.
segments.s164-g164: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessive ش). گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became'/'turned'. پیچان is an adjective meaning 'turning/turbulent' describing the state of the heart. به تیمار اوی literally 'toward care for him' — به + تیمار indicates the object of his solicitude, and اوی again is the possessive 'his' (i.e. 'care for him'). Together: 'his heart turned troubled with care for him.'
segments.s164-g165: ببارید is the past 'it rained/fell' used metaphorically of blood. خون is 'blood' (object/what fell). زنگه here is a poetic word referring to metal/metallic gear (literally 'ring/peal' but often used for metal objects or their sheen); شاوران is 'the advisers' (شاوران = counsellors/advisers) — together this phrase evokes the advisers' metal/armour being stained or affected by the blood.
segments.s164-g166: بنفرید is a verbal/adjectival form 'turned purple / became purple' (here describing the colouring by blood). بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. بوم in epic usage often means 'ground/land' (here 'the surface of the plain'). هاماوران is a poetic plural 'level plains' or 'the wide even land'. The sequence describes the plain being dyed purple by the blood and the sheen of metal.
segments.s164-g167: پر از غم is an adjectival phrase 'full of grief' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', غم = 'sorrow'). نشستند is the past plural 'they sat down' or 'they took their seats' (here: they sat in grief). هر دو 'both' and به هم 'together/with one another' emphasize that both acted together.
segments.s164-g168: روانشان is 'their spirit/soul' (روان + possessive -شان). ز connects cause 'because of/from' and governs گفتار او 'his speech/words' (گفتار = 'speech', او = 'he'). شد is the past 'became' and دژم is the adjective 'sullen/grim' — 'their spirits became sullen because of his words.'
segments.s164-g233: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” زنگه (zange) is the sound/toll (here the trumpet/bell sound) and is the subject of the verb. بیامد is past tense “came”; the verb appears after its subject as is normal in classical Persian. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase (به + نزدیک + شاه) indicating direction/location “near the king.”
segments.s164-g234: سپهدار is the subject (the commander). برخاست is a simple past verb meaning “stood up/rose.” از پیشگاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “from (the) presence/court” (از = from; پیشگاه = the royal presence or audience chamber). Word order places the verb after the subject and the source phrase.
segments.s164-g235: گرفتش is a past-tense verb + object suffix (گرفت + ـش) meaning “he took/seized it.” به بر تنگ is an adverbial phrase: به + بر + تنگ literally ‘to/upon [his] tight (breast)’ i.e. “close to his chest.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بنواختش is the past of نواختن with object suffix (بـ- prefix + نواخت + ـش) meaning “he sounded/played it.”
segments.s164-g236: گرامی is an adjective/adverb meaning “honored/precious.” بر خویش is a prepositional phrase here meaning “upon himself” (بر = on/upon; خوی + ش = his own/self). بنشاختش is a past verb with object suffix (بنشاخت + ـش) meaning “he set it up/placed it.” The sequence describes two actions: he both played the object and then made it his honoured possession.
segments.s164-g237: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” بنشست is the simple past “he sat down.” با شاه is “with the king” (با = with; شاه = king). پیغام داد is a verb phrase where داد is the past of دادن meaning “gave” and پیغام is the direct object “a message” — together “he sent/gave a message.”
segments.s164-g238: سراسر سخنها is a noun phrase meaning “the whole/entire speeches/words” (سراسر = entire; سخنها = speeches/words). بدو is the prepositional pronoun ‘to him.’ کرد یاد is a light‑verb construction: کرد (to do/make) + یاد (remembrance) yields “he reminded him / he caused memory” or more naturally “he recounted/related to him.” The object comes before the light verb, as is common.
segments.s164-g239: چو again functions as “when.” بشنید is past tense “he heard.” پیچان is a predicative adjective (lit. “twisting/turning” or “agitated/deceptive” here rendered as “agitated/angry”) and شد is the past of شدن “became,” so the clause reads “Afrasiab became (پیچان)”: i.e. he was seized by agitation/angriness. The name افراسیاب is the subject/affected person.
segments.s164-g240: دلش (دل + ـش) is “his heart.” گشت is a past/coprial verb meaning “became/turned.” پر + درد is a predicative phrase “full of pain” (پر = full (of); درد = pain/sorrow). و links the second parallel clause سر پر ز تاب where سر = head, پر = full (of), ز = from/because of, تاب = agitation/sway; literally “and his head became full from agitation.” The structure uses two parallel nominal predicates after گشت/گشت: “his heart became full of pain and his head full of agitation.”
segments.s164-g169: بدو (to him) is a prepositional/pronominal element placed before the verb for emphasis (‘to him’). باز is an adverb ‘again’ modifying گفتند. گفتند is a past plural verb ‘they said’ (here introducing the reported clause). کاین is the demonstrative/relativizer ‘that/this’, and رای نیست is the predicate ‘(this) opinion/counsel is not [acceptable]’ with نیست as the negating copula at the end.
segments.s164-g170: ترا is the object pronoun تو + enclitic را (‘you’/‘to you’), fronted for emphasis. بی‌پدر is a descriptive adjective ‘fatherless/orphan’ modifying the person. در جهان جای نیست is a locative existential phrase: در جهان ‘in the world’, جای نیست ‘there is no place’ — literally ‘there is no place in the world for you’. The final نیست is the negative existential copula.
segments.s164-g171: یکی is an indefinite subject ‘someone/one’. نامه بنویس is a jussive/imperative construction: نامه (object) + بنویس (2nd-person imperative or jussive ‘write/let write’) meaning ‘have someone write a letter’ or ‘let one write a letter’. نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase ‘near/to the king’ indicating where or to whom the action should be directed.
segments.s164-g172: دگر باره is a temporal/adverbial phrase ‘again/once more’ (دگر = again, باره = occasion/time). زو is a contracted poetic form of از او ‘from him’ (ز = از ‘from’; the following element in the given list is not the usual pronoun entry, so the tokenization is irregular). پیلتن را is the definite object marked by را (‘Piltan’ as the thing/person requested). بخواه is the imperative/jussive ‘ask/request’ (command form). Together: ‘Ask him again for Piltan.’
segments.s164-g173: اگر introduces a conditional clause ‘if’. جنگ فرمان دهد is a terse conditional: جنگ (war) + فرمان (command) + دهد (3rd-person present/jussive ‘gives’ or ‘utters’) — ‘if a command for war is given’ or ‘if war issues command’. جنگ ساز is an elliptic/imperative phrase meaning ‘make/prepare war’ or ‘be ready for war’; the verb is compressed into ساز (poetic/instructional usage), so the phrase calls for preparing for battle.
segments.s164-g174: مکن is the negative imperative ‘do not’. خیره is an adjective/participial ‘staring/astonished’ used adverbially (‘do not stand gaping’). اندیشه دل is a genitive phrase ‘the thought of the heart’ (اندیشه = thought, دل = heart). دراز is an adjective ‘long’; the phrase means ‘do not let the heart’s thought be long’ — i.e., do not hesitate or dwell too long.
segments.s164-g175: مگردان is a negative imperative ‘do not turn (away/against) / do not make’. به ما is the beneficiary/affected phrase ‘upon/against us’. بر is a preposition ‘upon/against’. دژم is an adjective ‘sullen/grim’ modifying روزگار. روزگار here functions as ‘time/fate/fortune’ — together دژم روزگار = ‘grim fate’ or ‘sullen fortune’. The clause pleads ‘do not make grim fate fall upon us.’
segments.s164-g176: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when/as’. آمد is the past-tense verb ‘came/happened’. درخت بزرگی is a nominal phrase ‘a great/large tree’ (درخت = tree, بزرگی = largeness/greatness used adjectivally). به بار is a verbal phrase meaning ‘to bear fruit / to come into bearing’ (in classical Persian به بار آمدن = ‘to bear fruit’); بار here is the ‘load/burden/fruit’ sense used metaphorically. The whole clause: ‘when a great tree came to bear (fruit).’
segments.s164-g177: نپذرفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'refused / did not accept' and here functions as the main verb. زان is an archaic prepositional/relativizing particle meaning 'of those' (literally 'from that') and immediately precedes the noun phrase دو خردمند پند, where خردمند (an adjective/noun 'wise one') modifies پند ('advice/counsel') which is the object of نپذرفت.
segments.s164-g178: دگرگونه is an adverb/adjective 'different/otherwise' modifying راز; بد is an adjective 'bad/ill' also modifying راز, so 'a different, bad secret'. راز is the head noun 'secret'. چرخ بلند is a compound noun phrase 'lofty wheel/sky' (چرخ here understood in the poetic/fateful sense 'fate/sky') with بلند 'high/lofty' describing it.
segments.s164-g179: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed expression meaning 'thus gave an answer' (چنین 'thus/so', داد 'gave' used here as the verb, پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the content clause that follows ('that/that…'), and فرمان شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's command' (noun-noun juxtaposition marking possession/dependency).
segments.s164-g180: برانم is a volitive/performative form meaning 'I will/shall cause (to be)/carry out' (classical usage of a verb of doing in first person). The following که again introduces the content clause; برتر ز ... is a comparative phrase 'higher/superior than …' where ز is the classical preposition 'than/from' and خورشید و ماه are the compared items 'sun and moon.'
segments.s164-g181: ولیکن is a conjunction 'but/yet'. به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' (به + noun), and دلیر is the adjective 'brave' functioning as the predicate modifier that links to the following verb; Persian allows the predicate/adjective to appear before the verb.
segments.s164-g182: نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive/impersonal form 'is not / should not be' (classical negative of بودن). ز ... تا ... marks a range 'from X to Y' (ز = 'from', تا = 'as far as / up to'); خاشاک 'chaff' is the lower end, پیل 'elephant' and شیر 'lion' the upper end, joined by و 'and'.
segments.s164-g183: کسی is the indefinite pronoun 'someone/anyone'. کاو here is an archaic relative particle meaning 'who/that' (not the verbal sense listed in the provided senses). ز فرمان یزدان is 'from God's command' (ز = from), and بتافت is an older/poetic verb meaning 'turned away/strayed' (the listed senses do not capture this meaning). Together the segment means 'anyone who turns away from God's command'.
segments.s164-g184: سراسیمه شد is a two-word predicate 'became frantic/distraught' (سراسیمه 'frantic', شد past of شدن 'to become'). خویشتن را نیافت is a negative past construction 'did not find himself' (خویشتن 'himself', را the definite object marker, نیافت past negative of یافتن 'to find'); together: 'became distraught and did not find himself.'
segments.s164-g241: بفرمود is a 3rd-person past/formal verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (an honorific past). تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here ('that/so that'), followed by جایگه (a concrete 'place' or 'seat') and ساختند, a past plural verb 'they built/made'; the clause therefore means 'he ordered that they make a place/seat.'
segments.s164-g242: ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'for him/to him'; چون here means 'as' (i.e., 'as was fitting'); سزا functions as 'desert/what is fitting' (classical sense 'that which is proper'); بود is the past copula 'was'; بنواختند is past plural 'they played/performed' (often said of musicians). Together: 'for him, as was fitting, they played.'
segments.s164-g243: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; پیران is the plural noun 'the elders'; بیامد is past 'came/arrived'. تهی کرد جای is literally 'made the place empty' (کرد as causative/making + تهی 'empty' + جای 'place'), i.e. 'he vacated his seat.' Word order is typical older Persian: verb (بیامد) follows the subordinate conjunction, and the causative construction places the object (جای) after کرده.
segments.s164-g244: سخن رفت is an impersonal idiom 'the talk went/turned' (رفت functioning like 'turned to/concerned'); با marks the target/topic 'with/about'; نامور کدخدای is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned kadkhoda (village headman).' The verb رفت here does not mean physical 'go' but 'became the subject of discussion.'
segments.s164-g245: ز (زِ) here is the poetic preposition 'of/about' ('concerning Kavus'); کاووس is the proper name Kavus. وز is an archaic/fused form (effectively 'وِ ز' = 'and of') not captured by the glosses given, so no listed sense fits exactly. خام گفتار او is a noun phrase 'his crude/uncouth speech' (خام 'raw/naive' modifying گفتار 'speech').
segments.s164-g246: ز again marks 'about/of'; a sequence of nouns joined by و lists his negative attributes: خوی بد 'bad disposition', رای 'plan/counsel' (here 'counsel/decision'), and پیگار (an older word for 'strife/battle' or 'contention'). او is the possessive 'his.' The series explains further topics of the conversation.
segments.s164-g247: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/kept (on)'; گفت here functions as the verb 'he was saying/kept speaking' (the provided dictionary senses were nominal, so the verbal sense is used in context). و links the next clause; رخساره کرده دژم is a causative/perfect construction: کرده (made) + adjective دژم ('sullen/grim') applied to رخساره ('his visage/face') — 'he made his face sullen' or 'his face grew sullen.'
segments.s164-g248: ز introduces cause/subject matter 'because of' or 'concerning' the affair of سیاووش (Siavash). کار is 'affair/matter' here; دل 'heart' followed by پر ز غم 'full of grief' is a fixed pattern: پر 'full (of)' takes ز + noun to indicate what it is full of. The whole clause states his heart was filled with sorrow over Siavash's affair.
segments.s164-g209: چو at the start is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when'. پاسـخ (object) + چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying یافت, the past tense 'found/received' (یافت is past of یابیدن). از introduces the source 'from', and نیکخواه is a noun 'well-wisher' (agent/source). The whole clause is a temporal clause: 'When (he) found/received such an answer from the well-wisher...'.
segments.s164-g210: چنین گفت is a typical classical/perfect narrative formula: چنین (thus) + گفت (past 'said'). با here functions instrumentally/causally 'by/with' and links to the following noun phrase: زنگه 'a peal/clang' (the sound) and بیدار 'awake/roused' form a participial phrase modifying شاه 'the king' — i.e. 'the king, awakened by a ringing/peal'. So syntactically: 'thus said (the king), the king having been roused by a peal.'
segments.s164-g211: The clause begins with که which introduces reported speech/command. رو here functions as an imperative 'go' (classical Persian رو from رفتن used as an order). شاه توران is a genitive phrase 'the King of Turan' (شاه modified by توران). سپه را is the definite object 'the host/army' marked by را, and بگوی is a jussive/imperative form of گفتن 'say/tell' placed at the end as the finite verb: 'Go; tell the King of Turan the host.'
segments.s164-g212: The second that-clause gives the content to be reported. زین is the archaic/formal demonstrative 'this' (classical form of این) modifying کار 'affair/deed'. ما را is the indirect object 'to us' or 'for us'. چه آمد is an interrogative/explanatory 'what came / what has happened' (آمد here = 'befall/happen'), and بروی (subjunctive/imperative form of رفتن) closes the clause as an instruction 'go [and tell him]'. Together: '...tell him what has happened to us because of this affair.'
segments.s164-g213: ازین is a compound prepositional/demonstrative 'from this' (here 'this' = the truce). آشتی جنگ is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the truce (آشتی) of war (جنگ)'. بهر (بهرِ) means 'for/for the sake of' and منست is a predicative adjective/verb phrase (archaic -ست) meaning 'is fitting/serves me' — overall: 'This truce is, for the sake of war, fitting/advantageous to me.'
segments.s164-g214: همه نوش تو is 'all your pleasures/delights' (نوش here = delight/pleasure). The predicate درد و زهر منست links two painful images 'pain and poison' as the speaker's reaction; منست again 'is to me / suits me' with ironic/negative sense: 'are to me pain and poison.' Word order is subject (همه نوش تو) + predicate (درد و زهر منست).
segments.s164-g215: ز here is the preposition 'by/from' (poetic). پیمان تو 'your covenant/promise' is the genitive phrase that governs the result-clause. سر نگردد تهی is an idiomatic sequence: سر (literally 'head', idiomatically 'honor/status') + نگردد (negative present/future sense of گشتن/گردیدن 'does not become/turn') + تهی 'empty/in vain' — together: 'by your pledge the head will not be left empty' i.e. 'your promise will not be in vain.' Note the archaic verb form نگردد for 'does not become.'
segments.s164-g216: وگر is the conditional 'and if / but if'. دور مانم: دور 'distant' + مانم (1st‑person present/jussive of ماندن) 'I remain' → 'if I remain distant'. ز تخت 'from the throne' (ز = from, تخت = throne). مهی is an adjectival epithet (literally 'moon‑like', often used poetically for beauty/royal splendor) modifying تخت — 'the moon-like throne' or simply 'the throne'. The clause expresses willingness to remain removed from the throne: 'and if I must remain far from the throne...'.
segments.s164-g89: بدیشان (= به ایشان) is an older fused prepositional phrase meaning “to them,” followed by چنین “thus.” گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” کز is the poetic contraction of «که از» (= “that/from”), introducing the content clause; بخت بد = “ill fortune.” The whole segment sets up reported speech: “He said to them thus, that from ill fortune…”.
segments.s164-g90: فراوان is an adverb “abundantly/often.” همی is the classical progressive/stative particle (marks continuous or habitual aspect) placed before the verb. بر تنم is a prepositional phrase “upon my body/me” (بر = on/upon; تنم = “my body” with possessive -م). بد is the nominal “evil/misfortune,” and رسد (final verb) is the present/past-form of رسیدن here used impersonally: “to befall / happen.” Word order is typical classical Persian with the verb at the end and preverbal aspect particle همی.
segments.s164-g91: بدان مهربانی is an archaic/fused locative/causal phrase (= به + آن = “by/ because of that kindness”). دل شهریار is a genitive construction meaning “the heart of the king” (notice the ezāfe is dropped in verse). Together the segment means “by that kindness, the heart of the king …” and supplies the topic for the simile that follows.
segments.s164-g92: بسان = “like/as.” درختی is an indefinite noun “a tree.” پر از is a fixed collocation “full of,” where پر precedes از + noun. برگ و بار is an idiomatic pairing meaning “leaves and produce/fruit” (برگ = leaf; بار = produce/load/fruit). The adjective-like phrase follows the topic and completes the simile: “(was) like a tree full of leaves and fruit.”
segments.s164-g93: چو = “when.” سودابه is the subject. او را is the object phrase “him” with the object marker را (here placed after او). فریبنده is a participial/adjectival form meaning “deceptive/enticing,” and گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copula/auxiliary “became.” The clause means “When Sudabeh began to entice/deceive him…” with O preceding the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s164-g94: تو گفتی is simple past “you said.” که introduces the subordinate content clause. زهر is “poison,” گزاینده is a participle/adjective from گزیدن meaning “biting/stinging” (here “stinging/venomous”), and گشت again is “became.” The subordinate clause reads literally “that poison became (a) stinger,” i.e. “that she became like a venomous/stinging thing.”
segments.s164-g95: شبستان = “bedchamber/inner chamber.” او following it marks possession (“her chamber”). گشت is the copular/auxiliary “became,” and زندان من = “my prison” (possession again expressed by the pronoun after the noun). The clause literally: “Her bedchamber became my prison.”
segments.s164-g96: غمی شد دل = predicate + verb + subject (an inversion common in poetry): غمی “sorrow” + شد “became” + دل “(the) heart” = “the heart became sorrowful” (i.e. sorrow filled the heart). و links the next phrase: بخت خندان من = “my smiling/cheerful fortune” (بخت = fortune; خندان = smiling). In this condensed classical phrasing the verb for the second noun is understood from the first clause.
segments.s164-g313: بدو (to him) is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb گفت (said); گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. پیران is the agent 'the elders' (subject). کاندر خرد is a genitival/locative phrase 'کان در خرد' = 'among/within the wise' and serves as the clause complement explaining the domain of what the elders say. The whole segment functions as 'the elders said to him that, among the wise...'.
segments.s164-g314: یکی is an indefinite 'one'/'a certain (one)'. شاه here is a noun 'king' and combines with کندآوران which appears as a compound/plural nominal (literary/archaic form) meaning roughly 'those who make/produce/make a king' (a 'king‑maker'); the exact dictionary senses given do not match this poetic compound, so the word is treated as a nominal. بنگرد is the present subjunctive/jussive third‑person singular of نگرستن 'to look/consider' (i.e. 'let/should he look' or 'he should consider/look upon'). The segment therefore means 'let him look to/choose one of the king‑makers.'
segments.s164-g315: کسی is an indefinite 'someone/a man'. کز is the contracted relative/preposition 'که + ز' = 'who/from' meaning 'who from'. پدر is 'father'. کژی 'crookedness/wrongness' and خوی بد 'a bad disposition' form a coordinated object (what might be inherited). The phrase as a whole sets up the condition 'someone who from his father (does not) take crookedness and bad disposition...'.
segments.s164-g316: نگیرد is the negative present/subjunctive/indicative form of گرفتن 'to take' here meaning 'does not take/receive' (the negative prefix ن- plus گیرد). ازو 'from him/of him' refers back to the father. بدخویی 'bad‑humour/ill‑temper' is the quality under discussion. کی is an interrogative adverb ('when/how' in rhetorical negative questions) and سزد is the impersonal/present form of سزیدن 'to befit' (i.e. 'it befits'/'it is fitting'). Together: 'If he does not inherit crookedness and ill‑disposition from his father, when/how is ill‑temper fitting (for him)?' (rhetorical: it is not fitting).
segments.s164-g317: نبینی is the negative second‑person present form of دیدن (literary: 'do you not see' or 'you do not see'); here it introduces an observation. که is the conjunction 'that' introducing the content clause. کاووس is the proper name 'Kavus'. دیرینه 'long‑standing/old' modifies کاووس, and گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became/has become'. The clause = 'Do you not see that Kavus has become old?'
segments.s164-g318: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. دیرینه گشت repeats 'has become old/long‑standing'. او 'he'. بباید is the impersonal/present modal 'must/ought' (literary 'it is necessary'), and گذشت (here a verbal noun/past participle sense) means 'to be passed over/set aside' (i.e. that he should be passed over). The clause therefore states the consequence: 'When he has become old, he must be passed over.'
segments.s164-g319: سیاوش is the proper name 'Siyâvash'. بگیرد is the present/subjunctive/epic-future‑like third‑person form of گرفتن 'to take/seize' (here 'will take' or 'shall obtain'). جهان فراخ is a noun phrase 'a spacious/wide world/realm' (جهان 'realm' modified by فراخ 'spacious'). The segment predicts Siyâvash's obtaining a large domain.
