segments.s164-g320: بسی is an adverb/adjective 'much/abundant' modifying گنج 'treasure'. بی‑رنج is a prefixed adjective 'without toil/without suffering' (بی‑ = without, رنج = toil/suffering). و links items in a list; ایوان 'iwan/hall/portico' and کاخ 'palace' complete the enumeration of what he will obtain. The word order lists possessions: 'much treasure without toil, and iwans and palaces.'
segments.s164-g137: چنین is an adverb meaning “such/like this” placed before the interrogative کی “who”; پسندد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive-form verb meaning “would approve/like.” ز من is a prepositional phrase “from me,” and کردگار is the vocative/name “O Creator” placed after the clause (poetic address without an explicit ای). The clause is a rhetorical question: “Who would approve of such (a thing) from me, O Creator?”
segments.s164-g138: کجا is the interrogative “where.” بر + دهد is a verb phrase in which بر (directional “to/toward/on”) combines with دهد (3rd-sing. “gives/places”) — here meaning “place/assign.” گردش روزگار is the agent/subject “the turning / wheel of (the) time/fate.” The phrase asks where the wheel of fortune will set/place the speaker.
segments.s164-g139: شوم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative of شدن (“may I become” / “let me be” / here introducing the speaker’s wish or resolve). کشوری is an indefinite noun meaning “a country/realm” (object of جویم). جویم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of جستن “I seek” (the verb of action). اندر جهان is a locative phrase “in the world.” The sequence literally reads “May I become; I seek a realm in the world.”
segments.s164-g140: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing the sought realm: “such that / where.” نامم is “my name” (the possessive -م is attached to نام; the provided split tags mislabel the suffix). ز marks the source/agent “from,” کاووس is the proper name Kāvus, ماند is the verb “remains/is left” (present sense “remain”), and نهان is the adjective “hidden.” The clause means “where my name remains hidden from Kāvus.”
segments.s164-g141: که here introduces a clause (“when/that”). روشن زمانه is a nominal phrase “the time/age was bright/enlightened.” بران سان is a compound adverbial/degree phrase “in that manner / so” (بران = on/thereon, سان = like/so), and بود is the past/copular verb “was.” Together: “when the age was so bright.”
segments.s164-g142: که again introduces a dependent clause: فرمان دادار گیهان is a genitival noun phrase “the command/decree of the Creator of the world” (دادار = the Creator, گیهان = the world), and بود is the copula “was/held.” The whole clause states that the Creator’s decree was in force/prevailing.
segments.s164-g143: سری کش is a compound agent (سری “secret” + کش “puller/extractor”) meaning “one who draws out secrets” or “secret-extractor.” نباشد is a negative existential/copular form here meaning “there is not / there is no” (classical usage). ز مغز آگهی = “knowledge/information from the brain” (ز = from; مغز = brain; آگهی = information/knowledge). The clause says that no one can extract hidden knowledge from the mind.
segments.s164-g144: نه (negation) + از بتری ... داند is a classical word order meaning “(he) knows the good from the worse,” here negated: “he does not know / cannot tell the good from the worse.” از marks the source/contrast (“from”), بتری = “the worse,” داند is 3rd-sing. present of دانستن “he knows,” and بهی is “the good/excellent.” The particle باز in this construction is archaic/contrastive and helps mark the comparison (hence treated as a small particle here).
segments.s164-g225: ز here functions as a causal 'because of' (poetic short form of از). خوی بد او is a possessive phrase: خوی (disposition) + adjective بد (bad) + enclitic او 'his'. سخن نشنوم puts سخن (speech, words) before the verb; نشنوم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive with negative ن- meaning 'I will not (hear/heed)'.
segments.s164-g226: Again ز = 'because of'. پیگار (battle/attack) is the cause; او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'his'. یک زمان ('one moment') is an adverbial phrase (زمان used as 'moment'). بغنوم is from غنود (to fall quiet/slumber); here it means 'be calmed/cease' or 'be at rest' in the speaker's first person (subjunctive/present sense).
segments.s164-g227: بشد is a past/preterite sense 'it happened / came to pass' (verb first as often in epic style). زنگه is the proper name (here the actor) followed by the instrumental phrase با نامور صد سوار 'with (his) famed hundred horsemen' — با + noun phrase marks accompaniment.
segments.s164-g228: گروگان is the direct object 'a hostage'. ببرد (برد in past form) is the verb 'took/carried off' (here past action). از در شهریار = 'from the gate/threshold of the king' — از + در (here 'در' as 'gate/door') indicates source/place.
segments.s164-g229: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در شهر = 'in the city', سالار ترکان is the noun phrase 'the commander/chief of the Turks' (noun + genitive). رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached', completing the temporal clause.
segments.s164-g230: خروش آمد is an idiomatic phrase 'a cry/uproar came/rose' (آمد = 'came/appeared'). و links the clauses. دیده‌بانش is 'his lookout/sentry' (دیده‌بان + 3rd-person possessive -ش) and بدید is the past verb 'saw' (he/it saw), so the clause reads 'and his lookout saw (it)'.
segments.s164-g231: پذیره here is a noun 'reception' or 'welcome', and شدش (شد + enclitic ش = 'befell him' / 'happened to him') forms a verbal periphrasis 'he was received/it befell him'. نامداری بزرگ is an appositive 'great renown' describing what befell him.
segments.s164-g232: کجا here functions rhetorically as 'where/so that' introducing a comment on his name; نام او is 'his name' but with the nuance of 'reputation'. بود is the past copula 'was'. جنگی طورگ pairs an adjective 'جنگی' (warlike) with طورگ ('manner/form'), so the clause says his name had a warlike character.
segments.s164-g201: همی + دید forms a continuous/past habitual sense: همی here is an adverbial marker meaning 'continually/still' and دید is the past tense verb 'saw/was seeing'. چشم بد روزگار is the subject phrase ('the evil eye of Fate') with the noun چشم ('eye') modified by بد ('evil') and روزگار ('Fate' here, not merely 'time'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (adverb + verb before subject).
segments.s164-g202: که introduces a subordinate/content clause ('that/what'), اندر نهان is a locative phrase meaning 'in secret' or 'hidden within', and چی ست is an archaic/question-like sequence where چی ('what') is followed by the copula ست ('is'). با + شهریار literally 'with the king' (here meaning 'in the king's presence' or 'concerning the king'). The clause order is 'in secret what is with the king'.
segments.s164-g203: نخواهد is the negative future/auxiliary (ن + خواهد) meaning 'will not' (here rendered as 'would not' or 'did not/would not see' in context). Poetic inversion places the verb before its logical subject: بدن نیز ('the body also') and دیدار او ('to see him') function together: literally 'will not (نخواهد) the body also see him.'
segments.s164-g204: ازان ('from that' / 'thereupon') refers back to the preceding event; چشم گریان شد uses شد as an inchoative/copular 'became' so 'the eye became weeping' = 'the eye wept' (گریان is an adjectival/predicative form). از کار او is a causal/ablative phrase ('because of his deed' or 'on account of his action') with از meaning 'because of' here.
segments.s164-g205: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used with the speaker's name). Word order is poetic: rather than 'زنگه چنین گفت' the adverb+verb precede the name. که introduces the reported speech clause 'that / that we are ...'. بنده‌ایم is the first-person plural present/stative 'we are servants' (بنده used as humble self-reference).
segments.s164-g206: به مهر سپهبد is a prepositional phrase 'with/for love of the spahbed' (به + مهر). دل آگنده‌ایم is a predicative construction: آگنده + ایم = 'we are filled' (دل آگنده‌ایم = 'our hearts are filled'). The verb is present/stative, declaring an ongoing affection.
segments.s164-g207: فدای تو بادا is an optative formula: فدای تو = '(be) sacrificed for you' and بادا is an older optative particle meaning 'may it be'. تن و جان ما ('our body and life') is the thing being offered; note the possessor 'ما' follows. The phrase expresses a fervent wish or vow.
segments.s164-g208: چنین باد is another optative formula 'may it be so.' تا introduces the duration 'until' and links to مرگ پیمان ما = 'our pledge (پیمان) [lasting] until death' where the verb 'be' is omitted but understood. The whole clause states the vow's intended permanence: 'so may it be until our death-bound pledge.'
segments.s164-g273: Two past actions joined by و: برآشفت is a perfective/past narrative verb 'he became angry,' followed by و + بگذاشت, another past verb 'he put/left (down)'. تخت و کلاه are its direct objects (verb then objects). کلاه here functions as 'crown/hat' (object) rather than a literal trick.
segments.s164-g274: همی + present-form جوید marks ongoing/habitual or narrative present 'he seeks/kept seeking'; از تو is the source/agent 'from you'; بدین گونه is the fixed classical phrase 'in this way' (made from ب + دین + گونه), and راه is the thing sought 'a way/manner'. Word order: verb (جوید) governs the following prepositional phrase.
segments.s164-g275: نه negates the impersonal clause نیکو نماید; نیکو نماید functions idiomatically as 'it is not proper/it does not seem right'. ز راه خرد is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by the way/method of wisdom' (ز + راه + خرد), giving the standard or counsel being appealed to.
segments.s164-g276: کزین is a fused classical form meaning 'from this' (که + ز + این); کشور is 'country/realm'; آن نامور is the demonstrative + adjective 'that renowned one'; بگذرد is a present/subjunctive/present-historical form 'should pass/leave' used in subordinate clause. Word order is relative/nominal: 'that [he] should pass from this country.'
segments.s164-g277: ترا here is the object 'you' (split in the word list into the 2nd-person suffix ت and the object marker را); سرزنش باشد is an impersonal/modal construction 'there will be reproach' or 'you will be reproached'; از مهتران gives the source/agents 'by the elders/notables'. The order places the object first for emphasis.
segments.s164-g278: سر او is a possessive phrase 'his head' (subject); همان is a demonstrative 'that same/likewise' referring back; از تو indicates cause 'because of you'; گردد is a classical present/subjunctive 'becomes' and گران is a predicative adjective 'heavy' (literally) used figuratively 'burdensome/troublesome'. The clause reads literally 'his head that same because of you becomes heavy.'
segments.s164-g279: و دیگر که is a temporal connective 'and later/after that when'; کاووس شد is a simple past 'Kavus became'; پیرسر is an adjectival epithet 'old-headed/gray-haired' meaning 'aged'. The clause states the temporal change of the king.
segments.s164-g280: ز تخت آمدش is a compact phrase 'from the throne came to him' where آمدش (آمد + ش enclitic) means 'befell him' or 'came upon him'; روزگار گذر is an absolute phrase 'the passage/passing of time' (روزگار = time/fate, گذر = passage). Together: 'the passage of time came upon him from the throne' — i.e., time removed him from the throne.
segments.s164-g153: ببر is the imperative (take!/bring!) form; همچنین is an adverb 'also/likewise'; جمله here means 'all, the whole lot'. تا ... پیش اوی is a prepositional/time phrase 'up to/before his presence' (تا + پیش + اوی), with پیش 'in the presence of' and اوی an archaic enclitic for 'him'. Together they form an instruction: 'take all (of it) and bring it before him.'
segments.s164-g154: بگویش is بگو + -یش (tell/say to him) — an imperative/jussive directive addressing the listener to speak to that man; که introduces a subordinate clause. ما را uses ما as the object 'us' with را as the definite/object marker. چه آمد به روی is an idiomatic verbless/periphrastic expression meaning 'what has come upon/what has befallen (us)', with آمد 'came/happened' and روی 'face' used figuratively.
segments.s164-g155: بفرمود is the past honorific/epic verb meaning 'he said/commanded'. The sequence بهرام گودرز را places the proper names with گودرز marked by را (definite/object marker), so the phrase functions to identify the addressee/topic (Bahrām) and the person concerned (Goudarz as object/topic of the remark). In narrative Persian word order is flexible here; the clause introduces whom the order or speech concerns.
segments.s164-g156: که introduces the content of the command/speech ('that...'). این 'this' + نامور 'renowned/famed' qualify لشکر 'army' and مرز 'border/frontier', with را marking them as the definite/direct object/topic that will be referred to in the continuation of the sentence (the verb completing the action appears later).
segments.s164-g157: سپردم is the past tense 'I entrusted/handed over' (subject understood from context). ترا (here written as ت + را) is the indirect object 'to you' ('I entrust to you ...'). The following nouns گنج و پیلان کوس list the things entrusted: 'treasure and elephants and drums' as direct objects of سپردم.
segments.s164-g158: بمان is the imperative 'stay/remain' addressed to the same 'you'. تا + subjunctive بیاید expresses a temporal 'until' clause ('until he comes'); سپهدار is the title 'commander' (the noun), and طوس is the proper name Tūs. Grammatically: imperative + تا + present subjunctive = 'stay until X comes.'
segments.s164-g159: بدو ده تو combines direction and imperative: بدو 'to him' (literally 'unto him'), ده (implicit here) is the imperative 'give', and تو 'you' is the vocative/imperative subject 'you (do it)'. این لشکر و خواسته names what is to be given: 'this army and the requested/desirable things'. The clause is an imperative command to hand these over to him.
segments.s164-g160: همه کارها 'all the affairs/matters' is the subject/topic, یکسر 'entirely/wholly' is an adverbial intensifier, and آراسته is a past-participial/adjectival predicate meaning 'arranged/ready/put in order'. Together they state that every matter has been fully prepared.
segments.s164-g185: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/always' emphasizing the action. دست یازید is the verbal idiom دست یازیدن 'to lay hands on; to attack'; یازید here is a poetic/archaic present/jussive form marking the action. باید is the modal 'must/ought' that scopes over the phrase, and به خون marks instrument/circumstance ('with blood' / 'in bloodshed').
segments.s164-g186: به کین introduces manner/means ('with/for vengeance'). دو کشور بدن is a noun phrase meaning 'the bodies of the two realms' (classical Persian permits the possessor/qualifier after the head noun). رهنمون ('guide') is predicative: the clause presents those bodies as the guide/indicator for vengeance.
segments.s164-g187: وزان پس که is a temporal/conjunctive sequence: وزان 'and from that', پس 'after/then', که introduces the subordinate clause. داند is 3rd-person singular present 'knows', and کزین کارزار means 'from this battle' (کزین = 'from this', کارزار = 'battle'). Together they set up the question about outcome.
segments.s164-g188: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative 'who/whom'. برکشد is a prefixed verb (بر‑ + کشد) meaning 'will lift/raise' (here future/optative force). گردش روزگار 'the turning/cycle of time/fate' is the grammatical subject causing the action; syntactically the clause asks 'whom the turning of fate will raise.'
segments.s164-g189: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / because of', نوا = 'melody/sound'. هم 'also/even' adds emphasis. بیازارد is a jussive/optative or habitual third-person form 'may annoy/afflict', and او is the third-person pronoun placed after the verb (poetic inversion) functioning as subject ('he').
segments.s164-g190: سخنهای گم‌کرده is a noun phrase 'the lost/forgotten words/speeches' (سخنهای plural + modifier گم‌کرده). بازآرد is a classical present/past-habitual form 'brings back / restores'; as above او after the verb is the subject 'he' (poetic word order).
segments.s164-g191: همان 'that very' picks out the previously mentioned ire. خشم و پیگار are coordinated nouns 'anger and battle/strife'. بار آورد is an idiomatic causative 'to impose / bring (a burden) upon' — here آورد is a perfective/preterite form 'brought/caused'.
segments.s164-g192: سرشک غم is a condensed genitive phrase 'a teardrop of sorrow' (سرشک = teardrop, غم = grief). اندر کنار 'into/at the side' = 'beside, in company with', and آورد (here) repeats 'brought'/'placed' (literal sense: he brought a tear alongside).
segments.s164-g297: برآساید is a jussive/optative 3rd‑person singular form meaning "may it be appeased" or "let it be at ease." "از کین دو کشور" is a prepositional/genitive phrase: از (from/of) + کین (hatred/vengeance) + دو کشور (two realms/countries). مگر at the end introduces an exception/qualification ("unless"). Word order places the optative verb before the phrase it affects.
segments.s164-g298: اگر begins a conditional clause "if/unless." آردش is آرد (3sg bring) with the enclitic object -ش ("him/it"), here functioning as the verb of the condition; in classical/poetic Persian آرد can have a subjunctive/conditional force. نزد ما is a prepositional phrase "to/near us/in our presence." دادگر is the agent noun "the just one/just ruler" and is the logical subject (or vocative) linked to آردش; the phrase reads "if the just one brings him to us."
segments.s164-g299: ز is the poetic preposition "by/from" introducing an agent or reason. داد جهان‌آفرین is a genitive phrase "the justice of the World‑Creator" (داد = justice; جهان‌آفرین = world‑creator). این سزاست is a nominal predicate: این (this) + سزاست (it is deserved/it is fitting), with سزاست functioning as a copular/predicative expression meaning "this is deserved/appropriate."
segments.s164-g300: که introduces a subordinate clause "that...". گردد is a 3rd‑person singular subjunctive/present of شدن/گردیدن meaning "become/come about." زمانه is the subject "the age/epoch." بدین is the prepositional/adverbial phrase "by this" (here printed as two items; ب is the prefix/particle used for "by/with" and دین should be read as part of the demonstrative "-دین" = "this"). جنگ is the instrument "war," and راست is the predicate adjective "right/just/straight" — together: "that the age become right by this war."
segments.s164-g301: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." سالار is the subject "the commander/leader." گفتار پیران is a noun phrase "the speech/words of the elders" (گفتار = speech; پیران = elders) functioning as the object. شنید is the simple past verb "he heard." Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal conjunction + subject + object phrase + verb.
segments.s164-g302: چنان هم is an adverbial cluster meaning "thus/so indeed." همه بودنیها is the object "all the beings/things that are" (همه = all; بودنیها = existents/that-which-is), and بدید is the past tense verb "he saw/perceived." The clause means he perceived that all such existences were so; verb follows the (compound) object as usual.
segments.s164-g303: پس is a sequential/contrastive particle "then/afterwards." اندیشه کرد is a verb phrase "he thought/pondered" where اندیشه is the noun "thought" and کرد is the auxiliary/verb "did/made" forming the past action. اندر آن یک زمان is a locative/time phrase "in that one moment/in that single instant" (اندر = in; آن یک زمان = that one time).
segments.s164-g304: همی داشت is an iterative/imperfective past construction meaning "he kept/continued to hold" (همی = still/continually + داشت = had/kept). بر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase "with regard to good and bad" or "about good and bad." بر گمان adds another prepositional phrase "in conjecture/speculation" — together: "he kept both good and bad in his conjecture (he weighed them in speculation)."
segments.s164-g441: Literal order is 'everyone toward Bahram you-have face' — a common classical Persian word order. همِه + سوی + بهرام sets the direction/address ('Everyone, toward Bahram'); دارید (present, 2nd person plural) combines with روی (face) in the idiom روی داشتن = 'to turn/hold one's face (toward)'. The clause therefore means 'All of you turn your faces toward Bahram.'
segments.s164-g442: می‌پیچد is the present 3rd-person singular (mi‑ prefix for imperfective), here meaning 'turns' or 'is turning'. دل را marks 'the heart' as the affected object (را = definite object marker). ز marks cause/agent ('from/by'), so ز گفتار اوی = 'by his speech' (اوی is the possessive/pronoun 'his'). The whole clause reads 'the heart is turned by his words' or more naturally 'his words make the heart turn.'
segments.s164-g443: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/still' marking ongoing or repeated action. بوسه دادند is a past 3rd-person plural verbal phrase 'they gave a kiss' — idiomatically 'they kissed'. گردان here is an epithet 'revolving' (poetic) modifying زمین 'the earth/ground', so the phrase names the object kissed: 'the (revolving) earth' (i.e. the ground). Word order places the object phrase after the verb in poetic style.
segments.s164-g444: بران is a contracted/poetic locative/relational element meaning 'upon that' or 'before that' and links the following epithet and title to the action (i.e. they kissed the ground before that person). خوب سالار is a descriptive phrase 'that good/fine commander' (خوب modifying سالار). باآفرین is an adverbial phrase 'with praise/acclaim' indicating how they performed the action (they kissed with praise/acclaim).
segments.s164-g321: This enumerates possessions with the Persian object-suffix + object marker pattern: 'دو کشور ترا باشد' = 'two realms be to-you' (i.e., 'will be yours'). 'ترا' is the enclitic second-person object (to/for you) separated here as 'ت' + 'را', and 'باشد' is the classical optative/present form 'be' (narrative/irrealis flavor in poetry). The coordinating 'و' links the compounds 'تاج و تخت' (crown and throne) to the things that will be yours.
segments.s164-g322: The clause comments on attainability: 'چنین' = 'such' (indicating the things just named), 'خود' is emphatic ('itself' / 'indeed'), and 'که یابد' = 'that (one) attains' (یابد is the present/subjunctive/optative-form meaning 'attain/obtain'). 'مگر' here works as a limiting particle 'except/only if', and 'نیک‌بخت' is a noun 'the fortunate person' — so the whole means that such gains are obtained only by the fortunate.
segments.s164-g323: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'; 'بشنید' is the simple past 'he heard'; 'افراسیاب' is the subject name placed after the verb (typical poetic word order); 'این سخن' is the object 'this speech/these words'.
segments.s164-g324: 'یکی رای' = 'one/a certain counsel/plan' (یکی here 'a certain' or 'one'); 'با دانش' modifies the counsel ('with knowledge', i.e., wisely); 'افگند' is an archaic past 'cast/laid' (here figurative: 'devised' or 'devised and set in motion'); 'بن' (literally 'root'/'foundation') functions metaphorically as the object — 'he cast/laid a (foundational) counsel'. The verb is past third-person.
segments.s164-g325: 'دبیر جهان‌دیده' is a noun plus epithet ('the worldly/experienced scribe'); 'را' marks him as the definite object; 'پیش خواند' (compound verb) = 'he called/summoned before (him)': 'پیش' = 'in the presence of / before', 'خواند' = past 'called/summoned' (here 'to appear before').
segments.s164-g326: 'زبان برگشاد' literally 'he opened his tongue' (زبان as 'speech/utterance' here, برگشاد = 'opened'); the coordinating 'و' links the second clause 'سخن برفشاند' where 'سخن' = 'speech/words' and 'برفشاند' (past) is a figurative verb 'scattered/sprinkled' — i.e., he began speaking and cast forth his words. Word order places the noun phrase before its verb, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s164-g327: 'نخستین' = 'first' (ordinal/adverbial); 'که' introduces the subordinate clause ('who/when'); 'بر خامه بنهاد دست' has inverted/poetic order: literal 'upon the pen he placed his hand' — 'بر' = 'on/upon', 'خامه' = 'quill/pen', 'بنهاد' (archaic past of نهاد) = 'placed/laid', and 'دست' = 'hand' (the thing placed).
segments.s164-g328: 'به عنبر' indicates the instrument/substance ('in/with amber' — here a fragrant/precious ointment or ambergris); 'سر خامه را' is the definite object 'the tip/head of the pen' (را marks the object); 'کرد مست' = 'made (it) intoxicated' — 'کرد' is causative 'made', 'مست' an adjective 'drunk/intoxicated', used figuratively to mean the pen was steeped/imbued with amber. The clause is past narrative.
segments.s164-g337: خداوند (Khoda-vand) is repeated as an optative/invocative nucleus: classical Persian often places the divine name before the quality wished for, implying 'May God be (the source of)...' or 'May God grant...'. Here the pattern is 'God + virtue' twice: 'God (of) modesty and God (of) fear' — the second خداوند governs باک. باک is a noun meaning 'fear/dread' (of God) and is coordinated with شرم by و.
segments.s164-g338: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/away from' introducing things to be averted; the clause is elliptical (the verb 'may He keep/grant' is omitted). بیداد and کژی are the evils to be averted ('injustice/oppression' and 'wrongness/crookedness'). دل و دست پاک is a coordinated noun phrase 'heart and hand (be) pure' with پاک as the adjective modifying both دل and دست.
segments.s164-g339: شنیدم is simple past first-person 'I heard'; پیام is the direct object 'tidings/message'. از ... تا ... is a fixed range expression 'from X to X' (here از کران تا کران 'from edge to edge', i.e. from one end to the other). Word order is verb – object – adverbial-range (typical classical Persian freedom of order).
segments.s164-g340: ز is again the poetic 'from' marking source of the tidings. بیدار دل is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the alert/awake heart(s)' (بیدار modifies دل). زنگه شاوران is a classical compound: زنگه 'ring/peal' used metaphorically as 'circle, assembly' and شاوران 'advisers' — together 'the ring/circle of advisers'. The whole phrase reads 'from the alert hearts of the circle of advisers.'
segments.s164-g341: غمی شد دلم is an idiomatic impersonal/personal expression: literally 'a grief came to my heart' → 'my heart was grieved' (شد here is the past of شدن). The clitic م attached to دل (دلم) marks possession 'my'. زانک ('زان که') is a classical conjunction 'because/since' introducing the reason clause that follows, and شاه جهان is the noun phrase 'king of the world' (subject of the following predicate).
segments.s164-g342: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following predicate. تیز شد is 'became sharp/harsh' (تیز here = severe/harsh rather than physically sharp); با تو means 'toward/with you' (i.e. in his dealings with you); اندر نهان is an archaic locative phrase 'in secret' (اندر = in, نهان = hidden/secret). Word order places manner/adverbial elements after the verb, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s164-g343: ولیکن is a literary connective 'but/yet'. به گیتی is a locative classical phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی). به جز is the fixed expression 'except/aside from'. تاج و تخت is a common pair 'crown and throne' (symbols of rulership). The overall phrase sets up the exception context: 'But in the world, besides crown and throne...'
segments.s164-g344: چه is the interrogative 'what' (direct object of جوید). جوید is the present-tense/gnomic 3rd-person form of جستن 'to seek' (here: 'does ... seek'). خردمند بیدار is a noun phrase 'a wise, alert man' (بیدار qualifies خردمند). بخت (here) functions as 'fortune/fate' used appositionally or as an emphatic noun often attached in classical Persian — the line is idiomatic: 'what does the wise, wakeful (one), O fortune, seek?' Practically it reads 'what does a wise, alert man seek?' — the sequence postposes the subject after the verb, which is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s164-g217: This is a nominal statement: جهاندار (the world-ruler/sovereign) is named and then qualified by a predicate phrase. یزدان پناه functions as a compound predicate ‘God (is) refuge’ and منست is an archaic/copulative ending that attaches the idea to the speaker (‘is for me’ / ‘is my’). Word order is Subject — predicate — copula (typical of poetic/classical Persian where the copula appears as a postfixed element).
segments.s164-g218: A parallel nominal clause: زمین تخت و گردون کلاه = ‘the earth (is) (my) throne and the sky (is) (my) crown’. The conjunction و links the two noun phrases; the final منست is the same archaic/copulative element that applies to both coordinated phrases, giving the meaning ‘are mine / belong to me’ in context. گردون is a poetic word for the heavens/sky and کلاه is used figuratively as crown.
segments.s164-g219: و دیگر introduces an additional point (‘and moreover’). که begins a relative/nominal clause. بر here is part of the later construction ‘رفتن بر شهریار’ but stands before خیره ناکرده کار which describes the person: خیره (astonishing/startling) modifies کار (deed) and ناکرده is the negative participle ‘not done’ — together meaning ‘one who has not done a striking deed’ (i.e. inexperienced or unproven).
segments.s164-g220: نشایست is the negative/impersonal form built on شایست (worthy/proper) with the prohibitive/impersonal sense ‘it is not fitting’. It governs an infinitive: رفتن (to go). بر شهریار means ‘to/into the presence of the king’ (بر + noun = before/unto). The construction means ‘it is not proper to go before the king.’
segments.s164-g221: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain / one (a)'; راه is the direct object 'a road/path'. بگشای is the 2nd-person imperative 'open' (poetic imperative form). تا introduces purpose/consequence and takes a present-subjunctive form: بگذرم is 1st-person present-subjunctive 'that I may/might pass'. Together: ‘Open a way so that I may pass.’
segments.s164-g222: بجایی is the compound/contracted form of به جای 'to the place' (here indicating destination). که introduces the relative clause that modifies that place. کرد is past tense 'made/did' with ایزد as the agent 'God'. آبشخورم is آبشخور 'watering-place' plus the 1st-person enclitic -م 'my' (treated here as one token), so the clause means 'that God has made my watering-place'.
segments.s164-g223: یکی again is an indefinite 'a certain/one'; کشوری (here) means 'a realm/kingdom' used as the object of جویم. جویم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive form (جویم = I seek / I would seek), and اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world'. The word order (object before verb) is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s164-g224: که introduces a purpose/relative clause: نامم is نام 'name' plus the 1st-person enclitic -م 'my' (= 'my name'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from', so ز کاووس = 'from Kavus'. ماند is the verb 'remain' (present/past sense 'remain, be left') and نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden'. The clause therefore means 'that my name remain hidden from Kavus.'
segments.s164-g249: فرستادن is the classical/perfective verb 'to send/dispatch' used here as the action 'he sent'. زنگه is treated as a personal name or a dispatched messenger (here the one sent). شاوران is the plural noun 'advisers' (object/recipients). Word order (verb + direct object/name + recipients) is typical of Ferdowsi's narrative compression.
segments.s164-g250: همه یاد کرد is a compressed classical expression: یاد کردن here functions like 'call/summon/mention' (i.e. 'he had all called/summoned'). از ... تا ... is a fixed distributive formula meaning 'from ... to ...' or 'from one edge to the other'; کران = 'edge/limit' and تا in this phrase means 'as far as / to'. The whole segment therefore means 'he summoned all from edge to edge.'
segments.s164-g251: بپرسید is a classical past/preterite-form function (the narrative past) meaning 'they inquired / asked'. کاین را uses کاین 'this/that' plus the object marker را. چه درمان کنیم contains چه 'what' and درمان 'remedy/cure'; کنیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative ‘shall we do / should we do’, so the clause is an indirect question: 'they asked what remedy we should pursue for this.'
segments.s164-g252: وزین here functions as 'و + زین' (and regarding this / and with this), introducing a second, closely related question. چاره جستن is an infinitival verbal noun phrase 'to seek a solution' (جستن = to seek). چه پیمان کنیم repeats the interrogative 'what' + پیمان 'agreement/plan/covenant' and the subjunctive کنیم 'shall we make', so the clause reads 'and in seeking a remedy, what pact/plan shall we form?'.
segments.s164-g253: بدو is the prepositional/allative pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb (classical word order: indirect object before verb). گفت پیران literally 'said the elders' (پیران = 'elders'); that combination introduces reported speech. که‌ای شهریار is a formulaic vocative 'that is, "O king"' (که here introduces the speech or clause and ای is the vocative particle).
segments.s164-g254: This segment is an elliptical line of direct speech. انوشه is a proper name/epithet (Anoushe). بدی here functions as part of that epithet or as an archaic/formal element attached to the name; in context it reads as a nominal phrase addressing or describing the addressee. تا بود روزگار is a temporal clause 'as long as the age/time lasted' (تا … بود = 'until there was / while there was'), qualifying the preceding epithet or assertion.
segments.s164-g255: تو از ما به هر کار داناتری follows the comparative pattern: تو 'you' + از ما 'than we' + به هر کار 'in every affair' + داناتری 'wiser' (comparative adjective formed with -تری). The order places the standard of comparison (از ما) immediately after the subject, a typical Persian comparative construction.
segments.s164-g256: ببایستها is the archaic/narrative modal 'it must / one ought' (ببایست is a necessity modal); the final -ها is an emphatic/poetic enclitic. بر here introduces the reason/ground 'because/on account of' and تواناتری is the comparative 'more able/more powerful'. Together: 'you must (decide) because you are the more capable.'
segments.s164-g281: This is a nominal predicate + coordinate: 'سیاوش جوانست' = 'Siavash is young', where جوانست is the older fused form of جوان است (copula -ست). The conjunction و links to the next clause; با فرهی is an ablative/instrumental phrase ('with/possessing nobility') that prepares the following verb.
segments.s164-g282: بدو is a dative ('to him'); ماند is a 3rd‑person verb meaning 'remains/is left' (classical present/past sense). In Persian the thing that 'remains' follows the verb: آیین و تخت مهی are the items that remain (compound nominal phrase), with مهی an epithet 'moon‑like/bright'.
segments.s164-g283: اگر introduces a conditional clause. شاه is the subject and بیند (here 'sees' in the sense 'considers/decides') is the verb. به رای بلند is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'by/with lofty counsel' (رای = counsel/plan, بلند = 'lofty'), modifying the verb.
segments.s164-g284: نویسد is a 3rd‑person verb 'he writes/will write' used here as the apodosis of the condition; Persian often uses simple present for this. یکی نامه سودمند places the numeral/indefinite 'one/a certain' before the noun (poetic inversion) and the adjective سودمند follows the noun as a postpositive modifier.
segments.s164-g285: چنان چون is a correlative conjunction meaning 'just as / as when'. نوازنده فرزند را places the agent noun and its object before the verb (poetic word order); را is the definite object marker attached to فرزند, even though the verb appears later.
segments.s164-g286: نوازد is a 3rd‑person singular verb here meaning 'soothes/plays for' (the second sense given). جوان خردمند را is the object group: جوان here functions as 'young (man/one)' with the adjective خردمند 'wise' following it; را again marks the definite object. The subject is implicit and parallels the agent introduced earlier.
segments.s164-g287: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'someone' (not simply the numeral). جای سازد is 'sets up/establishes a place/position' (سازد is 3rd‑person 'he makes/establishes'). بدین is the prepositional phrase 'in/into this' (here written with a b‑prefix and the following element), and کشورش is 'his country' with the possessive suffix ش.
segments.s164-g288: بدارد is a 3rd‑person verb meaning roughly 'he gives/assigns/has' (here best translated 'grants'). سزاوار is an adjective 'fitting/appropriate' functioning as the thing given or as a predicate applied to the thing given, and اندر خورش is a locative phrase 'in/among his food/provisions' (اندر = 'in', خورش = 'dish/provision').
segments.s164-g305: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis; داد is the simple past 3rd-person singular of دادن ('gave'); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer' following the verb. به marks the indirect object ('to/for'), and پیران پیر is a doubled/title phrase ('elders' + 'elder/guide') meaning the chief elder or the elders collectively. Word order is emphatic: adverb + verb + object + indirect object.
segments.s164-g306: که introduces a reported clause ('that'); هست is the copula 'is' in present form. اینک ('now' / 'at this moment') modifies the following predicate. گفتی here functions as 'what has been said' / 'speech' (classical usage), followed by همه ('altogether/entirely') modifying دلپذیر ('pleasing'). The clause is a quotation-content clause: 'that (what) you have now said is entirely pleasing.'
segments.s164-g307: ولیکن is a classical connective meaning 'but/yet'. شنیدم is the past 1st-person ('I heard'). یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (here 'a certain one') and داستان is the noun 'story/tale'. The phrase is a simple S-V-O clause with an indefinite object.
segments.s164-g308: که is the relative/complementizer 'that'; باشد is the classical present/subjunctive form of بودن often used as 'is' or 'may be' in narrative. بدین رای is 'in this opinion' (ب as the prepositional prefix + دین/این = 'this' in the manuscript split), and همداستان is an adjective 'agreeing/like‑minded' meaning 'in agreement with' the stated opinion. Together: 'that is in agreement with this view.'
segments.s164-g309: که/چون introduce a subordinate temporal clause 'that when/as'. بچه شیر نر is a noun phrase 'a cub (bache) of a male lion' where نر ('male') qualifies شیر ('lion'). پروری is a deverbal noun/gerund from پروردن meaning 'rearing/nurturing' (here 'when one rears/if reared'). The phrase sets the condition/time for what follows.
segments.s164-g310: چو (alternative form of چون) again 'when/as'; دندان is 'tooth' (here 'the teeth'); کند is the verb 'makes/causes' (3rd-singular) used idiomatically with دندان to mean 'its teeth become'; تیز is the predicate adjective 'sharp'. کیفر is the object 'punishment' and بری is an adjective 'wild/untamed' modifying the punishment (i.e. 'savage punishment'). The clause means: 'when its teeth grow sharp, it deals/inflicts savage punishment.'
segments.s164-g311: چو 'when' introduces another temporal clause. با زور و با چنگ are two coordinated instrumental phrases ('with force and with claw'); repetition of با is emphatic classical style. برخیزد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form of برخاستن ('to rise, to get up'); او is the pronoun 'it/he'. The verb follows the instruments and the pronoun appears after the verb (classical order).
segments.s164-g312: به is the preposition 'to/toward'; پروردگار here means 'nurturer/keeper' (rather than only 'Lord')—the one who reared the animal. اندر is a classical locative/into particle emphasizing direction ('into/on'); آویزد is the 3rd-person singular verb from آویختن meaning 'to cling/attach' or 'fasten upon'. او repeats the pronoun. Together: 'it clings to/fastens upon its nurturer.'
segments.s164-g385: یکی before a noun is the indefinite 'a/certain'; اسپ is the noun 'steed/horse'. بر سر is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the head (of)' that attaches to the following descriptive phrase. ستام گران is a compact adjectival description: ستام ('praised/ornamented') plus گران ('heavy/valuable') together yield the sense 'richly/remarkably adorned' or 'of great renown' modifying the horse's head or head-ornament.
segments.s164-g386: بیامد is simple past 'came/arrived' (3rd sing.). دمان (دامان) 'skirt/hem' here names the trailing robes or skirts of the attendants; زنگه is used as the sound-image 'jingle/peal' and شاوران is 'the advisers/courtiers'. The verb precedes the noun-phrase (a common poetic inversion): literally 'came the jingling of the courtiers' hems', i.e. their hems jingled as he arrived.
segments.s164-g387: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک تخت سیاوش is the locative phrase 'near Sīyāvash's throne' (نزدیک modifies the following noun تخت). رسید is the past 'reached/arrived' (3rd singular), so together: 'When (he) reached near Sīyāvash's throne...'.
segments.s164-g388: بگفت is past 'he said' (synoptic simple past). آنچ = 'that which/what' introduces the content of his speech; پرسید و بشنید و دید are three past verbs linked by و meaning 'asked, heard, and saw' — the sequence lists what he recounted. Grammatically, آنچ governs the coordinated verbs: 'he said that which he had asked, heard, and seen.'
segments.s164-g389: سیاوش is the subject. به یک روی is an idiomatic phrase 'on one face / in one respect' (به + numeral + روی meaning 'in one mood/aspect'). زان = 'from that' (referring to what was said/learned), and شاد شد is 'became happy' (شد = past 'became'). The line expresses a divided, two-sided emotional reaction.
segments.s164-g390: به دیگر = 'on the other (hand)/in the other respect'. پر از is the adjectival pattern 'full of' (پر + از), so پر از درد و فریاد شد means 'became full of pain and cries' (شد = 'became'). The clause contrasts the two simultaneous emotional states.
segments.s164-g391: که introduces the clause (here giving the content/reason). دشمن 'enemy' and دوست 'friend' are contrasted. همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (classical Persian). بایست here carries the modal sense 'ought/must' or 'should', and کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made', so the phrase expresses 'that an enemy should be treated/turned into a friend' (a desiderative or evaluative sense).
segments.s164-g392: ز is the poetic form of 'from'. آتش 'fire' is the source in the rhetorical question; کجا = 'where/how' introduces the impossibility question. بردمد (برد + مد) here means 'be carried/come forth' (past/imperfective poetic form) and باد سرد = 'cold wind'. The line asks rhetorically how a cold wind could come from fire (i.e. an impossible or unnatural outcome).
segments.s164-g353: کجا (question word) starts the clause meaning 'where'; من is the emphatic subject 'I' placed after the question word; گشایم is the 1st-person verb from گشودن (classical subjunctive/present form) 'I should/open (I open)'; در گنج بست is a noun phrase 'in/into the treasure (vault) that is closed' with بست functioning as a past/participle/adjectival 'closed/locked' modifying گنج. The segment asks where the speaker should open the locked treasure.
segments.s164-g354: سپارم is the 1st-person verb 'I entrust/give' (classical present/subjunctive form). به تو marks the indirect object 'to you'. تاج و تخت is the coordinated object 'crown and throne'. نشست here is a nominal/verb idea 'sitting' or 'to sit' (verbal noun or infinitive-like use) that links to سپارم: 'I entrust (to you) the crown and throne for (you) to sit (on)'.
segments.s164-g355: بدارمت is a fused classical form meaning 'I will keep you / I shall keep you' (verb + object enclitic). بی‑رنج is an adverbial adjective 'without pain / untroubled' modifying how the keeping will be done. فرزندوار is an adverbial simile 'like a child / childlike' describing the manner ('I will keep you harmlessly, as a child').
segments.s164-g356: به گیتی is a locative 'in/within the world'; تو is the pronoun 'you' (topic). مانی here functions as a verb 'you will remain / you shall remain' in classical/persian-poetic usage (note: the provided sense list gives proper‑name senses, so that sense does not appear and is marked below as unavailable). ز (poetic for از) + من gives the origin/comparison 'from/of me' (زمن = 'from me' or 'as regards me'); یادگار is the predicative noun 'a remembrance/memento'. The clause says that the addressee will remain in the world as the speaker's memento.
segments.s164-g357: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از کشورم 'out of/from my country' (از + country + possessor suffix م); بگذری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive (classical) 'you pass/will pass'; در جهان 'into the world' is a locative phrase showing destination. The clause sets the temporal/conditional frame: when you leave my realm for the world.
segments.s164-g358: نکوهش is the noun 'reproach/blame'; کنندم is a fused classical verb form meaning 'they do (to) me' — here 'they will reproach me', with the object enclitic م marking 'me'. کهان و مهان is a coordinated nominal phrase naming those who will reproach (i.e. important people), but the provided sense lists for کهان and مهان do not match the usage here, so those senses are marked unavailable.
segments.s164-g359: وزین روی is a prepositional/causal phrase 'by/with that face' (زین = 'from/this', here fused with و as poetic particle); وزین is here functioning to point to 'this/that' (classical poetic usage) while روی is 'face/appearance'. دشوار is the predicate adjective 'difficult'; یابی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'you find' (here meaning 'you will find'); گذر is the noun 'passage' or 'way'. The order is typical classical Persian: topical phrase + predicate + verb + object (i.e., 'With that face, you will find passage difficult').
segments.s164-g360: مگر is a concessive particle 'unless' (introducing the exception). ایزدی is an adjective 'divine'; باشد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of بودن 'there be / it be' (classical optative sense 'unless there be'); آیین is 'custom/rite/practice' and فر is the abstract noun 'royal glory / divine splendour'. The clause gives the exception: passage will be hard unless divine practice and glory favour it.
segments.s164-g265: This is a noun phrase introduced by the preposition به (here marking a sphere or quality: 'in/with respect to'). The three nouns are coordinated by و ('and'): بالا (loftiness, highness), دیدار (here 'bearing/appearance' or 'presence' rather than a literal meeting), and آهستگی (gentleness/softness). The order is typical Persian: preposition + list of nouns joined by و.
segments.s164-g266: A parallel noun phrase again headed by به, listing cultural/intellectual qualities: فرهنگ (culture/manners), رای (judgement/opinion), and شایستگی (worthiness). The repetition of به before شایستگی is poetic emphasis and links the two triads as comparable domains.
segments.s164-g267: This clause states the main comparison: هنر (skill/merit) با خرد (with wisdom) نیز (also) بیش (more) از نژاد (than lineage). Note Persian word order: the comparative بیش + از introduces the standard of comparison (here نژاد). با attaches خرد to هنر as a modifier ('skill with wisdom').
segments.s164-g268: This is an elliptical concessive phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز (ز = 'from/of'), saying 'from the mother (مادر) such as (چنو) a prince is born (شاهزاده نزاد)'. The verb is omitted (typical in poetry); the phrase qualifies 'lineage' — i.e. 'even if the mother were such that a prince was born of her.' نزاد is functioning as a nominal 'birth/origin'.
segments.s164-g269: An evaluative comparative: بدیدن کنون (seeing now) is a verbal noun phrase (infinitive used nominally) + temporal adverb کنون ('now'), followed by از شنیدن ('than hearing'). بهست (poetic form of 'it is good/enough' or 'it suffices / is better') serves as the predicate asserting the preference. Word order places the preferred activity first.
segments.s164-g270: A catalogue of epithets linked to the implied person: گرانمایه (precious/esteemed), شاهزاد (princely — an adjective here), و مهست (moon-like/beautiful). The verbless nominal phrases are typical in Persian descriptive lines: they complete the meaning of the preceding clause by characterizing the one seen.
segments.s164-g271: A conditional concessive phrase: وگر ('if/and if') + خود ('himself') + جز اینش ('except this') + نبودی (past subjunctive/optative—'were not') + هنر (skill). The phrase means 'and if he himself had no skill besides this' — note the verb نبودی is classical past-tense form used for hypothetical condition.
segments.s164-g272: The explanatory/countering clause introduced by که ('for/that'): 'که از خون صد نامور با پدر' — 'that (he) is/from the blood of a hundred renowned (men) with (on) his father.' از خون signals descent 'of the blood of', صد نامور quantifies the famous ancestors, and با پدر indicates the paternal line. The clause lacks an explicit copula but is understood to assert lineage.
segments.s164-g409: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. چشمش is 'his/her eye' (possessed noun; -ش = possessive). ز + دیدار + من is a prepositional phrase 'from/with the seeing of me' (ز = poetic 'from', دیدار = 'seeing/meeting', من = 'me'); گشت is the perfective/preterite-like verb 'became' (here 'grew, turned into'); سیر is a predicative noun 'sated/satiated'. Word order is typical Persian: temporal marker → subject (possessed) → prepositional phrase → verb → predicate noun.
segments.s164-g410: بر سیر is a prepositional phrase 'upon/with satiety' introducing the state; دیده ('the sight'/'the eyes') is the noun subject/experiencer; نباشند is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form of بودن here meaning 'do not remain' or 'are not long-lasting' (classical/poetic usage); دیر is an adverb 'long' placed after the verb. The clause thus says that the visual satisfaction does not last long.
segments.s164-g411: ز شادی is a prepositional phrase 'from/with joy'; مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'God forbid' and introduces the wish; دل او is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + او); رها here is adjectival/predicative 'freed/loosed' (rāhâ = unbound). Together the phrase means 'may his heart not be set free from joy' (i.e. may he not lose his joy).
segments.s164-g412: شدم من is 'I became / I was' with من as subject 'I'; ز غم is an ablative 'from sorrow/grief'; در دم اژدها is a locative phrase 'in/at the dragon's breath' (در = in/at, دم = breath/moment, اژدها = dragon), so the clause expresses that the speaker was undone/overwhelmed by grief at the dragon's breath. شدم is a simple past/resultative 'I became/was'.
segments.s164-g413: ندانم is the negated present 'I do not know' (classical form of 'نمیدانم'). کزین کار is a compound prepositional phrase 'from this deed/affair' (کزین = 'from this', کار = 'deed/affair'). بر من سپهر places سپهر ('the heavens'/'fate') after the phrase بر من ('upon me/with respect to me') in classical word order: literally 'I do not know from this deed what the heavens upon me ...' — i.e. 'I don't know what fate has in store for me because of this act.'
segments.s164-g414: چه is the interrogative 'what'; دارد is present 'has/holds' (what it has in store); به راز اندر is a phrase 'in secret / secretly' (به + راز + اندر = 'within a secret'); از کین و مهر is an ablative phrase 'from/pertaining to hatred and love' — listing possible motives. The clause asks what secret intention the heavens hold toward the speaker, whether out of enmity or affection.
segments.s164-g415: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that/thereupon', پس = 'after/then'). بفرمود is the honorific/past form 'he commanded/said' (polite classical past). بهرام را is the object marked by the enclitic را (definite/object marker): 'he ordered Bahram.'
segments.s164-g416: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'that / so that'; اندر جهان is locative 'in the world'; تازه کن is a two-word verbal instruction: تازه ('fresh/new') + کن (imperative 'do/make') = 'make fresh/renew'; کام را is the object 'the desire/wish' with را marking it. Together: 'that (you) renew/make the desire fresh in the world.'
segments.s164-g369: که introduces a subordinate/conditional clause here ('if/that'). ز ایدر به ایران literally 'from here to Iran' (ز = poetic 'from', ایدر = 'here', به = 'to/towards'), and شوی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/present of شدن used here to indicate 'you go/become (in) Iran'. با سپاه 'with an army' is an instrumental/comitative phrase attached at the end for emphasis; word order is typical poetic inversion (clause-marker → place phrase → verb → accompaniment).
segments.s164-g370: ببندم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive of بستن ('I will bind/fasten' or 'I will join/close'); here it functions idiomatically with راه to mean 'set out/join the road' or 'accompany on the road'. به دلسوزگی is an adverbial phrase ('out of compassion', constructed with به + the noun دلسوزگی). با تو marks accompaniment ('with you'). راه is the object/goal ('the road / way') and together with ببندم forms the idiom 'to bind the way with someone' = 'to go with someone'.
segments.s164-g371: نماند is a negative perfective/imperfective meaning 'did not remain / will not remain / there will be no ... left'. ترا (written here as ت + را) marks the person affected ('to/for you'). با پدر جنگ دیر literally 'a long war with the father' — دیر modifies جنگ as 'long (in duration)'. The clause means 'there will be no long war left for you with your father.'
segments.s164-g372: کهن شد is an adjectival phrase 'became old/ancient' (کهن 'old' + شد 'became'). سرش is 'his head' with the possessive enclitic ش ('his'); گردد is a 3rd‑person verb 'becomes/turns' used here in parallel with شد. از جنگ سیر means 'sated/tired from/of war' (سیر = 'sated, weary'); the whole line says his head grows old and he becomes sated of war. The sequence is a tightly compressed poetic clause: adjective + شد, possesive noun + verb, prepositional phrase indicating cause.
segments.s164-g373: گر is the conditional 'if'. آتش literally 'fire' is used here metaphorically (ardor/force of battle). ببیند is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of دیدن ('he meets/sees/encounters'). پی شصت و پنج places the timing 'after sixty‑five' (پی = 'after/following'; شصت و پنج = 'sixty‑five'); the time phrase follows the verb for poetic effect. Together: 'If (he) meets fire after sixty‑five...'.
segments.s164-g374: رسد is 3rd‑person singular 'reaches/comes' (here indicating result). آتش repeats 'the fire/ardor' as subject; از باد پیری is a causal/instrumental phrase 'from/by the wind of old age' (باد = 'wind', پیری = 'old age'), and به رنج ('into suffering') gives the result. The clause means the flame, driven by the gale of old age, results in suffering.
segments.s164-g375: ترا (ت + را) again marks the beneficiary 'for you'. باشد is an optative/subjunctive 'may it be / let there be' used to state a wish or grant. ایران و گنج و سپاه is a triad of nouns ('Iran, treasure, and an army') that are what 'may be yours'. The structure is a conventional 'may X be yours' formula in classical Persian.
segments.s164-g376: ز کشور به کشور is a repeated prepositional phrase 'from country to country / from realm to realm'. رساند is 3rd‑person singular 'brings/causes to reach' (used here as an assertion/wish about what will be done), and کلاه ('hat' in the literal sense, used poetically for 'crown') is the object. The subject is omitted (impersonal or 'he/they'), so the line says the crown will be conveyed from land to land.
segments.s164-g425: ز لشکر marks the source with the poetic preposition ز ("from") followed by لشکر "army/host". گزین کرد is a compound verbal expression: گزین (the root/participle meaning "choose/select") plus the past auxiliary کرد, giving a past action "he chose." سیصد سوار is the (numeral + noun) object referring to what was chosen; the line shows a somewhat inverted/poetic word order (prepositional phrase + verb + object) instead of a neutral SOV.
segments.s164-g426: همه ("all/everyone") emphasizes the entirety of the chosen men. گرد here is used adjectivally in a classical/poetic sense meaning 'gathered/together' or 'as a body' and links with همه; و connects to the next adjective. شایسته کارزار is a predicative adjective + noun phrase meaning "fit/worthy for battle" (شایسته modifying the following noun کارزار without an extra preposition). Together the phrase qualifies the three hundred horsemen as assembled and fit for combat.
segments.s164-g427: صد اسپ گزیده is a noun phrase: صد اسپ "a hundred steeds/steeds" with گزیده (past participle) postmodifying/spelling out that they are "chosen/selected." به + noun (به زرین ستام) here indicates accompaniment/with — 'with golden ...' — where زرین is the adjective "golden." ستام is a poetic/archaic noun (here meaning an accoutrement such as a saddle or trappings) qualifying what the horses had.
segments.s164-g428: پرستار here means attendants/caretakers (people attending the horses). و links items in a list. زرین کمر is an adjectival phrase 'golden-belted' or literally 'golden girdle,' and صد غلام is 'a hundred slaves/servants'—so the phrase describes a hundred attendants/servants wearing golden belts. The adjective-noun grouping (زرین + کمر) functions to modify غلام.
segments.s164-g429: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he said/ordered' (here functioning as 'he commanded'). تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause ('so that/that'), and پیش او آورند is that subordinate clause: پیش 'in the presence of/before', او 'him', and آورند is the present-subjunctive/plural form used after تا to express the commanded action ('that they bring'). Grammatically this is a control construction: the main verb issues the command and تا + subjunctive expresses the commanded content.
segments.s164-g430: This is a simple coordinate list of the things to be brought and then counted: سلیح 'weapons/arms', و marks the coordination, ستام (the same accoutrement word as before, here 'saddles/trappings'), و another conjunction, کمر 'belt/girdle'. بشمرند is the present-subjunctive/plural of شمردن used with the same purposive/subordinate structure: 'that they count/number them.' The verb refers back to the listed objects as its direct objects.
segments.s164-g431: درم نیز 'dirhams too/also' names the coin; چندان که is a relative/quantifier construction meaning 'as many/much as' or 'to the extent that.' بودش is the past tense 'was' with the enclitic -ش referring to 'to him/his' (i.e., 'as many as were to his use'), and به کار is an idiom meaning 'for use' or 'useful/needed.' Altogether it says 'also as many dirhams as were useful to him.'
segments.s164-g432: This short clause continues the enumeration of valuable things given: ز before دینار marks source/possession 'of/from dinars', and the following وز is a poetic fused form equivalent to 'و ز' (i.e. 'and from'); گوهر 'gems/jewels' names the precious items, and شاهوار is an adjective meaning 'regal/lofty' qualifying the jewels. Grammatically this is a compact poetic way to add 'and from dinars and (from) regal gems.'
segments.s164-g289: بر آیین is an adverbial prepositional phrase placed before the verb and means “according to custom/ceremony.” دهد is a 3rd-person present/historical form of دادن (“he gives”) governing the direct object دخترش را; را is the definite/object marker. بدوی here is an older spelling/orthography (or an enclitic-like form) and in this line functions as the recipient adverb (i.e. “to him/unto him”), but the printed lemma senses do not match this usage.
segments.s164-g290: بداردش is the verb بدارد with the enclitic object ش (her) attached: “he gives/lets him have her.” The verb is in the same narrative/present-habitual form as دهد. با ناز و با آبروی are two adverbial prepositional phrases (“with nâz and with âbru”) that describe the manner—with grace/affection (ناز) and with honor/reputation (آبرو).
segments.s164-g291: مگر here means “unless.” کاو in classical Persian often functions as an archaic third‑person pronoun (“he/that one”), not the verb senses listed; بماند is the 3rd-person present/habitual/subjunctive of ماندن (“he remains/stays”), and به نزدیک شاه places the action geographically/relationally: “near the king.”
segments.s164-g292: کند is the 3rd-person present/habitual of کردن used here as a future/performative: “he will make / he makes.” It governs the compound object کشور و بومت (country and your bõm—'بوم' with a possessive suffix meaning homeland/your soil) and the predicative noun آرامگاه: he makes them (into) a resting‑place/tomb. The verb thus takes two coordinated objects which share the predicate آرامگاه.
segments.s164-g293: و‌گر is a conditional conjunction “and if / but if.” باز گردَد is the verbal phrase meaning “returns / turns back” (باز = again; گردد = turns/returns in classical usage); سوی شهریار marks direction or destination “to/toward the king/sovereign.” The verb is in the 3rd-person narrative/subjunctive form.
segments.s164-g294: ترا is the old-style object form meaning “for you” (here segmented as ت + را in the word list); بهترِی باشد is an evaluative clause: بهترِی (بهتر + the nominalizing/relational ending) + باشد (subjunctive/optative “may be / will be”) yields “it would be better” or “it will be better.” از روزگار means “than/of fate/fortune.” The whole clause is a concessive/result: if he returns, it will be better for you than fortune otherwise would be.
segments.s164-g295: سپاسی بود is an impersonal/predicate phrase: سپاسی (praise/thanks) + بود (was) — “there was praise.” نزد شاه places that praise in the king’s presence or sight. زمین here denotes the land/country and functions as the theme receiving the praise.
segments.s164-g296: بزرگان گیتی is a genitive/attributive phrase “the great ones of the world.” کنند آفرین: کنند is the 3rd-person plural present/habitual of کردن (“they do/they make”) and here takes آفرین as its direct object meaning an acclamation/cheer — literally, “they make/utter ‘afarin’ (bravo).”
segments.s164-g401: ببایست is a modal/impersonal verb meaning 'it was necessary' (an archaic/poetic form); it introduces the necessity clause and takes a following verb phrase. بر + noun means 'over/on/across' (here 'over the mountain'), and گذشت is a past-tense verb 'passed'. Word order in Persian poetry can place the modal before the verb phrase: 'ببایست ... گذشت' = 'one had to pass'.
segments.s164-g402: This phrase shows poetic inversion: the object 'مرا' has been split into 'م' + object marker 'را'; 'را' marks a definite/accusative object. زار here functions as an adverb/adjectival idea 'mournfully/piteously'. بگریست is past 3rd-person singular 'he/she/it wept', and آهو (the subject) appears after the verb (typical poetic word order). به دشت is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/on the plain'.
segments.s164-g403: ازان is a causal/antecedent adverb 'from that/thereupon' linking back to prior disgrace; the coordinated ننگ و خواری 'shame and humiliation' names the motive. به + جنگ 'to war' marks direction/purpose, and آمدم is 1st-person past 'I came/went', so the whole clause means 'I went to war because of that shame.'
segments.s164-g404: خرامان is an adverbial participle/adverb 'striding/proudly' describing the manner. به + چنگ نهنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into the claw/grip of the whale' (چنگ = claw/grip, نهنگ literal 'whale' used figuratively). آمدم again is 1st-person past 'I came', so the clause reads 'I came, striding, into the whale's grasp.'
segments.s164-g405: دو کشور is the subject 'two countries/realms'. بدین آشتی is a prepositional/ adverbial phrase 'by/with this reconciliation' (note the printed segmentation is b + دین, but together بدین = 'by this'). شاد گشت is past 'became happy' (گشت = became), so the clause means 'the two realms became happy by this peace.'
segments.s164-g406: دل شاه 'the king's heart' is the subject; چون introduces a simile 'like/as', تیغ پولاد 'a sword of steel' is the image, and گشت is past 'became'. The phrase therefore reads 'the king's heart became like a steel sword.'
segments.s164-g407: نیاید همی is a negative-present/habitual construction meaning 'does not come/does not happen' or here 'never pleases'; همی gives a continuous/habitual sense. هیچ کارش = 'any/none of his works/deeds' (کار + possessive ش = 'his work'), and پسند is a noun 'approval/liking' used here predicatively. Together: 'None of his actions ever pleased.'
segments.s164-g408: گشادن is an infinitive/masdar 'opening' or 'to open' used nominally; همان و همان 'the same and the same' emphasizes sameness. بود is past 'was', and بند here is the poetic 'stanza' or 'verse-unit'. The clause states that the opening (and the stanza) was always the same.
segments.s164-g393: Word order is somewhat classical: subject 'یکی' (a certain one/someone) + object 'نامه' + past verb 'بنوشت' (wrote). 'نزد پدر' is a classical dative/locative phrase meaning 'to/unto his father' or 'in the presence of his father' (here: recipient). 'بنوشت' is past tense, 3rd person singular.
segments.s164-g394: 'همه یاد کرد' is a light-verb construction: the noun 'یاد' (remembrance/recounting) + past 'کرد' = 'recalled/related/recounted all'. 'آنچ' = 'that which / what' introduces the content recounted. 'بد در به در' is an idiomatic, emphatic phrase ('bad/ill — dar be dar') meaning 'wandering from door to door', i.e. destitute or driven about; here 'بد' qualitatively modifies the idiom.
segments.s164-g395: The clause begins with the subordinator/relative 'که' ('that' / 'for'), followed by subject 'من' and the circumstantial 'با جوانی' ('in/with youth') — classical placement before the predicate. 'خرد یافتم' is past tense 1st singular 'I found/obtained wisdom' (یافتم = 'I found').
segments.s164-g396: 'بهر' here means 'toward / for the sake of'; it governs the following nouns 'نیک و بد' ('good and bad'). 'نیز' ('also') emphasises inclusion. 'بشتافتم' is past 1st singular 'I hastened' — figuratively 'I strove/pursued' both good and bad (i.e. I hurried to experience or understand both).
segments.s164-g397: 'از آن زن' = 'from that woman'. The phrase 'یکی مغز شاه جهان' is compact and poetic: 'یکی' = 'a certain / one (of)', 'مغز' here means 'core/essence, a striking quality or jewel', and 'شاه جهان' ('king of the world') is a genitive apposition describing the kind/quality (i.e. an essence fit for a great king). Word order is inverted for poetic compression.
segments.s164-g398: 'دل من برافروخت' = 'my heart was kindled' (برافروخت is past tense, 3rd-person singular passive/intransitive meaning 'was kindled' or 'grew aflame'). 'اندر نهان' is a circumstantial phrase 'within/secretly' (اندر = in, نهان = hidden/secret), so the heart's kindling is internal and concealed.
segments.s164-g399: 'شبستان او' = 'her bedchamber/inner chamber' (possessive: شبستان + او). 'درد من شد نخست' is literal: 'became my sorrow first' — 'شد' is past 'became' and 'نخست' marks it as the initial/primary affliction. The clause states that the chamber was the origin/cause of his first pain.
segments.s164-g400: Poetic verbless/elliptical phrasing: 'ز خون دلم' = 'from the blood of my heart' (ز = from), where the possessive '-م' is attached to 'دل' (shown separately in the word list). 'رخ ببایست شست' = 'the face must be washed' — 'ببایست' (must/ought) + 'شست' (washed, past participial/past form used here with modal sense). The whole line means his face ought to be washed with his heart's blood (a hyperbolic expression of grief).
segments.s164-g417: سپردم is a past-tense, 1st-person verb 'I entrusted/handed over' placed at the start (poetic inversion). The enclitic ت is the 2nd-person object pronoun 'you' attached to the verb, while را is the definite object marker placed after the pronoun for emphasis: together they mark 'I entrusted you (as object)'. The nouns تاج و پرده‌سرای follow as the things entrusted; و simply joins them.
segments.s164-g418: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the very' qualifying the whole following noun phrase. گنج آگنده is a noun (گنج) followed by a postpositive adjective (آگنده = 'filled, overflowing'), a common order in Persian poetry. The sequence تخت و جای are additional, coordinated nouns ('throne and seat/place') completing the list of things entrusted; no copula is required or present.
segments.s164-g419: This is a coordinated list of martial elements: درفش (standard/banner), سواران (riders/horsemen), and پیلان کوس. The lack of a conjunction between پیلان and کوس links them closely: پیلان کوس functions as a compound idea ('elephant-drums' or drums associated with the elephants). و links the major items.
segments.s164-g420: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ایدر 'here' is an adverbial. بیاید is the 3rd-person present-subjunctive/optative form of آمدن used in temporal subordinate clauses to mean 'comes'/'arrives'. The noun phrase سپهدار طوس (commander Tous) appears after the clause — poetic inversion — so the subordinate temporal clause precedes the subject.
segments.s164-g421: چنین هم 'thus also' frames the action. پذیرفته is a perfect participle 'has accepted' (or 'accepted'); او را (him) is the object marked by را. سپار is an imperative 'entrust/hand over' taking the earlier object او را — the imperative follows the statement, a compact poetic command.
segments.s164-g422: تو is the vocative 'you'. بیدار دل is a descriptive phrase where بیدار ('alert') qualifies دل ('heart') — together 'of watchful heart' — and باش is the imperative 'be'. و links the following prepositional phrase به روزگار, where به + روزگار means 'with respect to the times' or 'regarding the present circumstances'.
segments.s164-g423: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. دیده means 'eye' and is the source of the action. ببارید (literally 'fell'/'poured down') is used for drops/flows, so the clause means 'from the eyes fell...'. خوناب زرد is a noun (خوناب 'blood-water, blood-tinged fluid') followed by the adjective زرد 'yellow/pale', postpositively describing the fluid.
segments.s164-g424: لبِ رادمردان is a genitive construction 'the lips of the nobles' (لب + possessive genitive رادمردان). پر (here 'full (of)') takes از to introduce what fills them: باد 'wind/breath' and سرد 'cold' modifies باد. The whole predicate says their lips/mouths were filled with cold breath/sighs.
segments.s164-g329: The phrase places the definite object 'جهان‌آفرین' (marked by the object marker 'را') before the verb. 'ستایش گرفت' is a light-verb construction in the simple past (گرفت) meaning 'received/was given praise' — poetically functioning like a passive ('was praised').
segments.s164-g330: 'بزرگی و دانش' is a compound nominal phrase (greatness and knowledge). 'نمایش گرفت' uses the noun 'نمایش' + the past verb 'گرفت' to mean 'was displayed / took on display'; the past verb is used without overt plural agreement, as is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s164-g331: 'کجا' opens a rhetorical question ('where'). 'برترست' is an older/poetic third-person singular copula meaning 'is superior' (the -ست ending is archaic/poetic). 'از' introduces the standard of comparison ('superior to'), followed by the two nouns 'مکان و زمان' (place and time).
segments.s164-g332: 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative). 'کی رسد' = 'when will ... arrive/reach' with 'رسد' in the present/subjunctive-type form meaning 'reach/attain'. The phrase 'بندگی را گمان' is a compact poetic order: 'بندگی را' (the phrase marked by را) modifies 'گمان' so the whole means 'the thought/conception of servitude' — i.e. 'When will the thought of servitude reach him?'
segments.s164-g333: 'خداوند جانست' is a short equational clause: 'خداوند' + the archaic/prefixal copula-like 'جانست' = 'God is life' (جانست = 'is life' / 'is dear' in older usage). The conjunction 'و' links this to the following noun phrase 'آن خرد' ('that wisdom').
segments.s164-g334: Here 'خردمند را' is the indirect/beneficiary phrase 'to the wise' (marked by را). 'داد' is the simple past 'gave' and 'او' ('he') is the pronoun subject (referring back to God) placed after the verb for poetic emphasis; 'پرورد' appears as an epithet/verb-form meaning 'the nurturer' or 'nurtured' — together yielding 'the nurturer gave that to the wise.'
segments.s164-g335: 'ازو باد' is an optative construction: 'ازو' = 'from him' and 'باد' is the wish/optative particle 'may it be'. 'بر شاهزاده درود' places the beneficiary/target with 'بر' + the noun 'شاهزاده', and 'درود' is the blessing/greeting that is wished upon him.
segments.s164-g336: This is an appositive enumeration following the wish: 'خداوند' (God) followed by a string of nouns 'گوپال و شمشیر و خود' functioning as roles or attributes (protector/guardian, sword, and himself/the very self). 'گوپال' is an archaic/epic epithet (literally 'cowherd' but used poetically as a guardian/protector), 'شمشیر' is 'sword', and 'خود' is emphatic 'the very self'.
segments.s164-g257: A coordinated list of four nouns joined by و (and), followed by the possessive pronoun من which attaches to the whole list: the sequence is noun1 و noun2 و noun3 و noun4 + من = “my X, Y, Z and W.” Word order is noun-final possessive rather than a separate possessive modifier before each noun.
segments.s164-g258: چنینست is a fixed copular/expletive phrase 'it is thus'; اندیشه is the noun 'thought/intention' that follows and is scoped by the adpositional phrase بر جای من ('concerning/regarding my place'). The preposition بر + جای (here 'rank/position') gives the topical object of the thought.
segments.s164-g259: An introductory relative clause introduced by که + هر کس که = 'whoever/anyone who'. بر نیکوی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in respect of goodness' or 'excellent in good deeds'; در جهان places the scope 'in the world'. The clause sets up the subject for the following predicate.
segments.s164-g260: توانا is an adjective 'capable/strong'; بود is the past/remote copula (here narrative or generalizing past: 'was/is'); آشکار و نهان are coordinated adverbial/qualifying words 'openly and hiddenly' (i.e., publicly and privately) modifying the predicate. Word order places predicate then adverbial modifiers.
segments.s164-g261: ازین شاهزاده is the prepositional phrase 'from this prince' (از + این written as ازین). نگیرند is a negative present/optative 3rd-person plural form meaning 'they do not/will not take' or 'let them not take'; باز functions as an adverb 'back/away' (i.e. 'take away/back'). The verb is negated and applies to the whole object phrase.
segments.s164-g262: زگنج and ز رنج are two coordinated sources introduced by the fused preposition ز(=از) 'from' + noun: 'from the treasure and from the toil/hardship'. آنچ آید فراز is a relative/nominal clause 'that which comes forth/on high' (آنچ = 'that which'; آید = 'comes'); فراز here is adverbial/poetic 'forth/on high' describing the coming. Together they form the object 'whatever comes from treasure or from hardship.'
segments.s164-g263: من is the subject 'I'. ایدون here is an archaic/adverbial element meaning 'until now' or 'hitherto' (classical usage); شنیدم is the past tense 'I heard' or 'I have heard', introducing the reported clause که اندر جهان. اندر جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase. (Note: the precise adverbial sense of ایدون is archaic.)
segments.s164-g264: کسی نیست is an existential negative 'there is no one'; مانند او 'like him' is a simile phrase, and از مهان 'among the great/nobles' specifies the class being compared. The negative copula نیست negates the existence of any person comparable to او in that class.
segments.s164-g377: پذیرفتم is past 1st-person singular (‘I accepted/received/assented’). از پاک یزدان is a source phrase ('from the Pure God'), where از marks origin. که introduces the subordinate clause (reported content) and من is the clause's explicit subject (placed before the verb in the next segment, a common ordering in classical Persian).
segments.s164-g378: بکوشم is the present/subjunctive 1st-person form ('that I strive / I shall strive'), used after که to state the intended action. به خوبی is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'for goodness' (به = 'for/to', خوبی = 'goodness'), and به جان و به تن repeats به to mark two parallel complements 'with (my) soul and with (my) body' (i.e. 'with all my heart and body').
segments.s164-g379: نفرمایم is the present (here negative) 1st-person form of فرمودن with the negative prefix ن- ('I will not say / I will not decree'). و links the two coordinated verbs. خود نسازم is 'I will not make myself' (خود = myself; نسازم = 1st-person present/subjunctive/negative of ساختن 'to make'). به بد marks the becoming/state 'into evil' (به + adjective meaning 'into/unto').
segments.s164-g380: به اندیشه here is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'by/with (regard to) thought' (به = 'by/with'), دل را is the definite direct object (را marks the object 'the heart'), and نیازم is a 1st-person present form of نیازیدن used here in the sense 'I set / I incline / I make (my heart) need' (contextually negative from the previous clause: 'I will not set my heart upon...'). The final به بد again indicates the direction/state 'toward evil'. Note the verb appears after its object, a frequent poetic ordering.
segments.s164-g381: چو marks a temporal clause 'when'. نامه به مهر اندر آورد is a verb phrase 'brought the letter in with its seal' (اندر آورد = 'brought in/presented', where اندر is an older prepositional particle and آورد is the past of آوردن). شاه (the king) follows the verb in this poetic word order (subject after verb).
segments.s164-g382: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he said/ordered'. تا introduces the content or purpose of the order here ('that/in order that'). زنگه is a proper name (here the addressee), which is not represented among the provided noun senses (so marked below as unknown). نیک‌خواه is an epithet/compound meaning 'well-wisher' or 'benevolent' describing that person.
segments.s164-g383: به زودی = 'soon/quickly' (adverbial). به رفتن is an infinitival/prepositional phrase 'for leaving / to depart' (به = 'for/towards' + رفتن = 'going'). ببندد is 3rd-person subjunctive/present form 'he should bind / he girds' (used here under the indirect command introduced by تا), and کمر is the object meaning 'girdle/waist' (i.e. 'gird his loins').
segments.s164-g384: یکی خلعت آراست = 'he adorned/arrayed one robe' (یکی = 'one/a certain', خلعت = 'robe of honour', آراست = past 'adorned'). با سیم و زر is an instrumental phrase 'with silver and gold' (با = 'with', و = 'and', زر = 'gold'), modifying how the robe was ornamented.
segments.s164-g361: بدین is the fused form of به + این meaning 'by/through this' and modifies راه ('road/way'). پیدانبینی is a negative present/ subjunctive form (نبینِ + ـی) addressing 'you' — here used in a general/conditional sense 'you do not / cannot find'. زمین (literally 'land' or 'ground') is the object: word order is topic (بدین راه) + predicate (پیدا نبینی) + object (زمین) in this poetic line.
segments.s164-g362: گذر کردن = 'to pass' is expressed here as گذر + کرد with باید as the modal 'must'. The line exhibits poetic inversion: instead of the neutral order باید گذر کرد به دریای چین, Ferdowsi places گذر کرد before باید. به دریای چین gives the destination 'to the Sea of China.'
segments.s164-g363: ازین (از + این) means 'by/through this'. کرد یزدان is a past-tense clause 'God made/did' where کرد is the verb and یزدان is the subject. ترا (ت + را) is the 2nd-person object 'you' (ت enclitic plus object-marking را). بی‌نیاز is the predicate 'free of need / self-sufficient' placed after the object: 'By this God made you self‑sufficient.'
segments.s164-g364: هم 'also/even' emphasizes the following command; ایدر 'here'. بباش is an archaic/hortative form meaning 'be/remain (you)' addressed to the addressee. و links the clauses; به خوبی literally 'in/with goodness' (i.e. 'in your good fortune'); بناز is an imperative 'be proud' (lit. 'take pride'). Together: 'Also stay here and be proud of your good fortune.'
