segments.s164-g365: This is a list of possessions: سپاه 'army' و در گنج 'and the treasure within' و شهر آن 'and that city'. تست here is the archaic copula/possessive predicate (equivalent to توست/تو راست) meaning 'is/are yours.' Word order places the owned items first and the copula at the end, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s164-g366: به رفتن 'to going / for going' marks the purpose ('an excuse for leaving'). بهانه 'an excuse/pretext'. نبایدت is the negative modal with a 2nd-person enclitic (نباید + ت) meaning 'you must not / there is no need (for you)'. جست (here) is the verb 'to seek' used after نباید: 'you must not seek an excuse to go' — i.e. 'do not look for a pretext to leave.'
segments.s164-g367: چو here introduces a conditional ('if/when'); رای 'plan/opinion/decision'; آیدت is the verb 'comes to you' with the 2nd-person enclitic (it literally reads 'a decision comes to you' = 'you decide'). آشتی با پدر is the object phrase 'reconciliation with your father.' So the clause means 'If the decision to reconcile with your father comes to you' (= 'if you decide to make peace with your father').
segments.s164-g368: سپارم is first-person (present/subjunctive/hortative) 'I will entrust / I will give' with implicit 'I' and the object ترا (ت = you) following it; را is the object marker. تاج و زرین کمر are the two things given: 'the crown and a golden kamar' (کمر here = girdle/belt). The verb expresses promise/offer contingent on the prior conditional clause.
segments.s164-g345: ترا is the enclitic object/dative (تو + را) meaning "for you"; این همه ایڈر is a noun phrase "all this here" (ایدر = archaic/higher-register for "here"); آراستست is the poetic/contracted form of آراسته است (past participle + copula) meaning "is adorned/arranged." Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the beneficiary ترا appears before the locative and predicate.
segments.s164-g346: اگر introduces a conditional "if"; شهریاری is an abstract noun "sovereignty/royal power" and here names what might be desired; و‌گر is an emphatic/alternative conditional particle ("or if / but if"); خواستست is an archaic past/optative form (from خواستن) used here to mean "wished/desired" with an implicit subject (you or in general). Together this gives the sense "if (you) wish for sovereignty."
segments.s164-g347: همه شهر توران is a genitive/grouping phrase "all the cities of Turan." برندت is برند + ت (3rd-person plural verb 'they carry/bring' with the enclitic object -ت = 'you'); نماز is the object 'prayer' and here means 'prayers offered to you.' The verb is placed before its object in this poetic order (برندت نماز = 'they will bring/offer prayers for you').
segments.s164-g348: مرا ... باشد نیاز is an older/syntaxically marked way to say "I will be in need" (literally "there is to me a need"). م (here representing an existential/auxiliary element from هست) + را is the object marker attached in the old word order, خود "myself" emphasises the speaker, به مهر تو = 'for/for the sake of your love/affection', باشد is the (optative/formal) copula 'may be/is to be', and نیاز is the noun 'need/longing.' Together: 'I myself shall be in need of your love.'
segments.s164-g349: تو فرزند باشی uses باشی (present/subjunctive/optative 2nd sg of بودن) to state 'be (you) a child' or 'you shall be a child'; و links clauses; من چون پدر = 'I like/as a father' with چون meaning 'like/as.' The clause sets up a relational pairing (you = child, I = father) by using the subjunctive-like باشی for a courteous/prospective command or desiderative statement.
segments.s164-g350: پدر پیش فرزند بسته کمر is a compact past-stative clause: پدر is the generic/focal 'father,' پیش فرزند = 'in the presence of / before the child' (پیش = 'in front of / before / in the presence of'), بسته is the perfect/past participle of بستن ('bound/tied, i.e. girded'), and کمر 'waist/loins.' Literally 'a father, before his child, has girded his waist' = 'the father has girded his loins for his child.' The verb phrase appears after the preverbal phrase in poetic order.
segments.s164-g351: چنان دان is an imperative or hortative 'consider/know it so' (چنان = 'thus/so', دان = 'know/consider'). که introduces the following content clause. کاووس is the subject; بر تو = 'toward you' (بر = 'toward/upon'), and به مهر = 'with affection/for love' specifying motive or attitude. Word order places the directive 'چنان دان' before the explanatory clause.
segments.s164-g352: بران گونه = 'in that manner' or 'in that kind/way' (بران = 'thereon/thence', گونه = 'manner'); یک روز gives the duration 'one day'; نگشاد is the negated past form (ن + گشاد) meaning 'did not open / did not show'; چهر is 'face/appearance.' Literally: 'in that manner for one day he did not open/show his face,' i.e. 'he did not reveal his face that way even for a day.' Poetic word order places the negated verb before the noun it applies to.
segments.s164-g433: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' (ازان = 'thereupon' + پس = 'then/after'); گرانمایگان را is the definite direct object (را marks the object) — گرانمایگان is a plural noun 'the illustrious/esteemed ones' (nobles, dignitaries). The verb بخواند is a 3rd person singular past-form (classical narrative past) with the prefix ب‑; here it means 'he summoned/called' (a poetic/older usage of خواندن = to call).
segments.s164-g434: سخنهای is the plural construct 'words/speeches' (سخن + ـها/-ی for plural/construct). بایسته is an adjective 'fitting, appropriate' modifying سخنها. چندی here means 'a few / some' (quantifying the following noun). براند is a 3rd person singular past-form of راندن with a b‑ prefix; in this poetic context it functions as 'he sent forth / uttered' (i.e. 'he delivered' the words).
segments.s164-g435: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت is the past 3sg of گفتن 'to say' used here as a narrative verb). کز is the classical contraction of که + ز meaning 'that/from which' and introduces the subordinate clause. نزد افراسیاب means 'in the presence of Afrasiab' (نزد here = 'in the presence of / at the court of'; افراسیاب is the name).
segments.s164-g436: گذشتست is a perfective/past construction 'has passed / passed by' (گذشت + ـست); پیران is the subject 'the elders'. The sequence ب + دین in the wordlist corresponds to the classical particle بدین ('to/unto this' or 'to this side'); روی آب literally 'the face/side of the water' = 'this side of the river/sea' (روی = 'surface/side' or 'upon', آب = 'water'). Together this clause says the elders have passed to this side of the water.
segments.s164-g437: یکی here means 'a certain' / 'someone' (introducing an indefinite person). راز پیغام is a noun phrase 'a secret message' (راز = 'secret', پیغام = 'message'). دارد is the present/past narrative form 'has' (3sg of داشتن used to mean 'possesses/has'). به من marks the recipient 'for me' (به = 'to/for', من = 'me').
segments.s164-g438: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. ایمن is an adjective 'safe, secure'. به is the preposition 'with/by/with the help of' here linking the following numeral phrase (literally 'safe by/with two hundred'); از marks origin/agency 'from / away from'; انجمن is 'the assembly / council' — so the clause reports that he is safe with two hundred (men) away from the assembly.
segments.s164-g439: همی سازم is a first-person singular phrase 'I make / I will make' where همی is an older/progressive particle (emphatic or iterative) and سازم is 'I arrange / I prepare' (ساختن). اکنون is 'now', and پذیره شدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'the reception / being received' (پذیره = reception, شدن = nominalizer/infinitive 'to become / to be'). The clause functions as 'I will now arrange the reception'.
segments.s164-g440: شما را هم 'you too' (شما = 'you', را marks the direct object and here has a topical/emphatic role; هم = 'also'). ایدر is an older form of 'اینجا' meaning 'here'. بباید is a modal auxiliary 'must / ought' (3rd-person form used impersonally or for 'you must' in context). بدن is written as the sequence here but in context functions as the verbish element meaning 'to stay/be present' (in poetry such forms can be contracted); the whole clause therefore instructs that 'you must also be here / stay here.'

sentences.s164-s5: Sovereign over Bahram and Kayvān and the Moon; sovereign over good and ill, over farr and rank.
sentences.s164-s6: By his command is the revolving heaven; from him the domain of Mehr is spread everywhere.
sentences.s164-s7: To you, O youth: may health and fortune remain always with crown and throne.
sentences.s164-s8: If my counsel grows dark upon your heart, then from the sleep of youth your head will be stunned.
sentences.s164-s1: He outfitted the beast; King Kavus commanded that he return to the road.
sentences.s164-s2: The scribe read the letter aloud before them and set his body upon a golden throne.
sentences.s164-s3: He dispatched a letter—full of wrath and battle—(for) his tongue was sharp and his countenance like many‑colored.
sentences.s164-s4: First he praised the Creator, the Lord of repose and of battle.
sentences.s164-s25: Give Tūs's army the refusal and return; you are not a man to make an assault on the day of battle.
sentences.s164-s26: You mingled with the fair‑faced at the banquet and fled from the field of battle.
sentences.s164-s27: They set the king's seal upon the messenger; he spurred the beast into full gallop and took the road.
sentences.s164-s28: When the letter reached Sīyāvaš, he saw in it that sort of unpleasant wording.
sentences.s164-s97: If your hearts grow dark because of my actions, turn your heads away from my words.
sentences.s164-s98: Let me be your own messenger and remain your guide on that stage-like plain.
sentences.s164-s99: When Siavash gave such an answer, the lives of the two proud ones withered again.
sentences.s164-s100: They wept for fear of separation; when they were roasted on the fierce fire, they were consumed.
sentences.s164-s33: A hundred Turkic men and horsemen — famed thus among the king's kin — said (so).
sentences.s164-s34: If I send them before the king, they are all well‑wishers and all innocent.
sentences.s164-s35: He neither asks nor considers their affairs; at once he has them hung alive on the gallows.
sentences.s164-s36: What excuse shall I make before God? Ill will will come to me because of my father's deed.
sentences.s164-s73: Qobad came and went and handed the world over; he too must be reckoned among the gone.
sentences.s164-s74: O famed one, ornament of the advisers, adorn the body with heavy toil.
sentences.s164-s75: Go to Afrasiab's court; do not linger, and do not let your head fall into sleep.
sentences.s164-s76: The hostage and this request — whatever it may be — were settled by dinars and by crown and throne.
sentences.s164-s61: The two worlds will take (them) from me; may I remain to Ahriman’s heart’s desire.
sentences.s164-s62: Would that my mother had not borne me; or if the birth of death comes upon me...
sentences.s164-s63: For that many a calamity must be endured, and from the world one must taste poison (i.e. suffer bitterness).
sentences.s164-s64: By this manner the covenant that I have made, I have sworn it by God and by oaths.
sentences.s164-s17: The king's reputation in the realm was that he sought to be without the sword.
sentences.s164-s18: When Tus the spahbed comes before you, he will deal with you as is proper — sometimes less, sometimes more.
